output: Go to the Greengenes2 release page and pull the files that are similar to those outlined there. Then proceed with RESCRIPt. Also see this.

instruction: Account for uneven sampling depth per month when calculating monthly Phyla abundance for a stacked barplot of ASV relative abundance data in R.
input: ASV relative abundance data, R
output: Consider performing a power analysis to demonstrate sufficient replicates. Normalizing sampling depth is debated; the 2014 McMurdie and Holmes paper argues against rarefying. Instead, use bootstrapping (for example, the q2-boots plugin in Qiime2) to account for uneven depth.

instruction: Determine whether q2-kmerizer can merge 16S data from different regions for taxonomic analysis and suggest alternative methods if it cannot.
input: q2-fragment-insertion, q2-kmerizer
output: q2-kmerizer cannot merge 16S data from different regions for taxonomic analysis. It provides a pseudo-phylogenetic diversity estimate approach, but the kmer profiles differ between regions so it cannot be used for comparing data from different regions. An alternative is closed-reference OTU clustering. The 2025.7 distribution of the amplicon workflow still includes fragment-insertion; otherwise closed-reference OTU clustering is the most similar alternative that will remain available.

instruction: Use Qiime2 to process 16S rRNA data from 200 samples grouped by process type, ensuring primers that differ in base pairs are handled so that ASVs are compatible and microbial communities can be compared across processes.
input: 16S SRNA, 200 samples, Qiime2
output: If the only differences are some base pairs, you can process the whole dataset together. Make sure that the primers end with the same DNA sequence to ensure that ASVs will be compatible. Compare primers, make that they end at the same position, delete primers separately for each batch / sequencing run, run Dada2 also separately, but with the same settings. Then you can merge feature tables and representative sequences.

instruction: Determine whether the current QIIME pipeline steps are correct for fungal phylogenetic diversity analysis, and explain if the OTU-based approach is better, including the need to merge reads properly.
input: qiime tools import, qiime vsearch dereplicate-sequences, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, qiime2 classifier, vsearch pipeline, OTU-based approach
output: You should merge forward and reverse reads properly using tools such as vsearch merge-pairs; avoid concatenating reads with "NNNN" spacers. Once the reads are merged, you can follow the OTU clustering tutorial. If you only have fasta files, note that they lack quality scores, so you cannot perform full quality control in QIIME2. A practical approach is to copy all forward fastq sequences into one file and all reverse fastq sequences into another, ensuring consistent labeling and ordering, then import these into QIIME2 and use vsearch merge-pairs. The OTU-based approach is the recommended workflow for this type of data.

instruction: Import taxonomy so it can be added to a classifier with the rescript data.
input: qiime tools import --input-format HeaderlessTSVTaxonomyFormat, rescript evaluate-fit-classifier, ValueError: Taxonomic label depth is uneven. All taxonomies must have the same number of semicolon-delimited ranks.
output: To import taxonomy correctly, use the HeaderlessTSVTaxonomyFormat with a single column that contains a semicolon‑delimited string of taxonomic ranks. Ensure that each row has the same number of ranks and that there is no trailing semicolon or extra spaces after the semicolons. Removing the trailing semicolon from each taxon string resolves the uneven‑rank error and allows the classifier to be built successfully.

instruction: Explain how to resolve the taxonomy mismatch error that occurs when collapsing a QIIME feature table at genus level after filtering unwanted lineages.
input: qiime taxa filter-table, qiime taxa filter-seqs, qiime taxa collapse, --p-exclude list, --p-level 6, taxonomy mismatch error
output: The error shows that the taxonomy file is missing taxonomic labels for some features in the table, indicating a mismatch that likely happened before filtering. One way to confirm this is to collapse the taxonomy before applying the filter; the same error should appear. The safer approach is to filter sequences and the feature table together using the QIIME feature-table commands, e.g., qiime feature-table filter-seqs to remove unwanted sequences and qiime feature-table filter-features to keep only features that have matching taxonomy entries.

instruction: Identify the best database to use for training 16S (bacteria), 18S (Apicomplexa and Kinetoplastida), and 28S (nematoda) classifiers.
input: qiime tools import, qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, PR2, SILVA
output: Use `qiime rescript get-pr2-data` to fetch PR2 files (e.g., version 5.0.0) for training 18S classifiers. SILVA contains both 16S and 18S SSU genes and also 23S/28S LSU sequences, so it can be used for 16S, 18S, and 28S classifiers. The upcoming QIIME 2 release will allow pulling PR2 version 5.1.0.

instruction: Explain how to resolve the scikit‑learn version mismatch error when using the SILVA V3 classifier in QIIME2‑amplicon‑2025.7, including whether an officially supported classifier exists, how to retrain a classifier, recommended reference sequences, and future releases.
input: scikit-learn, QIIME2-amplicon-2025.7, SILVA V3 classifier, feature-classifier
output: Use RESCTIPt to retrain a SILVA classifier that matches the scikit‑learn 1.4.2 version in your environment. Follow the tutorial examples for amplicon‑specific classifiers and extract the V3–V4 region from the SILVA 138‑99 NR sequence file with `qiime feature-classifier extract-reads`. For forward‑only data you can also use a full‑length SILVA classifier; it is generally acceptable and will not be negatively impacted by incomplete read length. If you need a region‑specific classifier and have limited memory (≤16/24 GB RAM), build one; otherwise a full‑length classifier is fine. There is no official SILVA V3 classifier for QIIME2‑amplicon‑2025.7, and it is unclear whether the QIIME2 team will release updated region‑specific classifiers in the 2025.x series.

instruction: How can I set the ngram range to [6,6] for the combined qiime rescript evaluate-fit-classifier, evaluate my custom UNITE classifier with RESCRIPt, and understand whether evaluate-cross-validate and evaluate-classifications evaluate the classifier or the database?
input: qiime2-amplicon-2025.7, RESCRIPt, q2-feature-classifier, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, qiime rescript evaluate-fit-classifier, evaluate-cross-validate, evaluate-classifications, evaluate-taxonomy, qiime quality-control evaluate-taxonomy, qiime quality-control evaluate-composition, ngram-range
output: evaluate-cross-validate is designed to simulate classifier performance on unseen sequences; it is slow because it splits the database into K folds, trains a new classifier K times on the remaining data, and classifies the test set with each in‑house classifier.  evaluate-fit-classifier trains a single classifier and classifies the same sequences, giving a best‑case estimate of performance.  The ngram‑range parameter is not exposed in the RESCRIPt wrapper for evaluate‑fit‑classifier, so it cannot be set to [6,6] without modifying the underlying code.  To evaluate a custom UNITE classifier, you can run a simple pipeline: use qiime feature‑classifier classify‑sklearn with your custom classifier on the same sequence file, then use qiime rescript evaluate‑classifications (or qiime quality‑control evaluate‑taxonomy) to compare the resulting classifications to the curated reference taxonomy; this gives the best‑case F‑measure.  evaluate-cross-validate and evaluate-classifications together assess both the classifier and the database: the F‑measure reflects how well the database resolves taxonomy when the classifier is applied to unseen data.  Cross‑validation typically yields a more realistic F‑measure than the best‑case estimate, while the best‑case estimate is the highest possible F‑measure if every query sequence is present in the training data.  Thus the two approaches provide boundary conditions, and the true performance on environmental samples lies between them.

instruction: Determine why feature-classifier classify-sklearn yields 100% unassigned taxonomy when rerunning with a custom UNITE classifier on the mock fungal ITS library, given identical ASV inputs and previous successful runs.
input: qiime2-amplicon-2025.7, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, --i-classifier custom-classifier_UNITEdb_20251122.qza, --i-reads asvIDs_mockITS_min2.qza, --p-confidence 0.98
output: Try using search‑based classifiers such as classify‑consensus‑blast or classify‑consensus‑vsearch; the BLAST classifier returned expected taxonomy for many ASVs. The likely cause is the orientation autodetector in classify‑sklearn misinterpreting read orientation. Set the --p-read-orientation parameter, e.g., to 'both', and rerun; this produced correct fungal classifications.

instruction: Troubleshoot the MemoryError when running qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn on a 24‑sample 16S Illumina MiSeq 2×250 dataset.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, silva-119-99-515-806-nb-classifier.qza, kale_rep-seqs.qza, memory error
output: The error is a MemoryError caused by insufficient RAM. The VM’s maximum allocation is 16 GB, but the usable limit is about 11 GB. Increase the VM’s RAM slider to 11–12 GB and retry the classify‑sklearn command. If the issue persists, consider using a lower‑memory classifier such as classify‑consensus‑vsearch or classify‑consensus‑blast, which also accept pre‑trained databases. For custom reference files, import them into QIIME artifacts as FeatureData[Sequence] and FeatureData[Taxonomy] before running classify‑consensus‑vsearch. Greengenes is smaller and works for many plant samples. If more RAM is required (e.g., 30 GB for the pre‑trained SILVA classifier), run the analysis on a machine with more memory, such as an Amazon EC2 instance or a local workstation with ≥30 GB RAM. Note that placing data in a shared host folder does not consume VM RAM.

instruction: Could I use the command biom-add-metadata to add my taxonomy to the feature table, and what happens with the sequences called "new sequences" from open‑reference analysis?
input: Qiime2, PGM Ion Torrent, 18S, Silva 128, blast, usearch, sklearn, Vsearch, biom, tsv
output: Yes, you can use `biom add-metadata` to attach taxonomy information to a BIOM table. In QIIME 2 you export the feature table and the taxonomy separately, then merge them with `biom add-metadata` outside of QIIME. Open‑reference OTU picking creates a set of new reference sequences; these appear in a column called "new_reference_sequences" and can be used in subsequent open‑reference runs. The new reference sequences do not automatically receive taxonomy—you must assign taxonomy to them separately if desired.

instruction: Determine why the same OTU is classified as Bacteria only in one sequencing center but fully classified in the other, given identical sequences, lengths, classifier, and primers, and that one center produced more reads.
output: Likely the issue is caused by the classify‑sklearn autodetector being fooled by a mix of read orientations in the dataset from center B. The autodetector chooses the orientation that gives the highest median confidence for the first 100 reads; if reads are in both forward and reverse‑complement orientations it can end up using the reverse‑complement path, which often results in only a kingdom‑level (Bacteria) assignment. You can verify this by running classify‑sklearn twice, once with --p-read-orientation same and once with reverse‑complement, and comparing the results. If both give a high proportion of k__Bacteria, the dataset likely contains mixed orientations. In that case you need to either trim the reads to remove the mixed portion, correct the orientation in the metadata, or use a different classifier (e.g., blast‑ or vsearch‑based) that is less sensitive to orientation. Additionally, the denoising step (e.g., dada2 trim‑left, trunc‑len parameters) can influence how many reads survive and therefore the taxonomic profile; adjusting these parameters can reduce the proportion of poorly classified reads. Check the denoising statistics and read quality plots to decide on appropriate trimming/truncation settings.

instruction: Troubleshoot why QIIME2 feature-classifier fails with the SILVA database when running in Docker, noting the error message and suggest how to resolve the issue.
input: docker, qiime, feature-classifier, classify-sklearn, OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device
output: The problem is with your system, not the classifier itself. The key error is "OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device", indicating that the container is running out of disk space. The SILVA database is larger than Greengenes and requires more memory and temporary disk space. It is likely filling up the temporary directory. Diagnose and fix by changing the temporary directory location. If changing the temp directory does not solve it, adjust the Docker disk size slider to allocate more space, assuming the host has sufficient space. After increasing the disk size, re‑run the original command without altering the temp directory.

instruction: Explain why qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn gives the error 'No space left on device' and how to fix it when using a 48GB RAM machine with 2TB HDD, a 1TB working directory, and the command qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn --i-classifier Reference/silva-132-99-nb-classifier.qza --i-reads clustering/close/rep-seqs-cr-97.qza --o-classification taxonomy/taxonomy_close_99.qza --verbose --p-n-jobs -1
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, p-n-jobs -1
output: The error comes from the way the SILVA classifier loads its database into memory. When you use --p-n-jobs -1, QIIME tries to run the job on all available cores. For each core it opens a copy of the SILVA database, which is about 32 GB in size. On a machine with 48 GB of RAM, running the job in parallel creates more memory demand than is available, causing the OS to return an ‘No space left on device’ or ‘cannot allocate memory’ error. To fix this, limit the number of parallel jobs (for example, use --p-n-jobs 1 or 2). You can also reduce memory usage by setting --p-reads-per-batch to a smaller value; the job will process fewer reads at a time. Make sure the TMPDIR environment variable points to a writable directory on the 2 TB drive (e.g., export TMPDIR=/home/user/tempo) and that it is not mis‑specified. With fewer parallel jobs and a correct temporary directory, the classifier should run without running out of memory.

instruction: Explain how to resolve inconsistent taxonomic classification when repeatedly running qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, where sometimes only 'unassigned' reads are returned.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, --p-n-jobs 12
output: The classifier may produce inconsistent results if it is unsure about the orientation of the query reads. The read orientation can be forced manually using the --p-read-orientation parameter. Run classify-sklearn again with --p-read-orientation set to the same (or reverse-complement) orientation. Verify that your query sequences are not in mixed orientations, that they are trimmed to the same sites as the reference sequences, and that they do not contain untrimmed primers or other non-biological sequences. If setting the orientation manually fixes the issue, no further steps are required.

instruction: Determine whether the QIIME 2 feature-classifier uses a default subset of 10,000 features when training on ~2 million arthropod reference sequences, explain why the classification output contains ~10,000 taxa instead of 2 million, and suggest parameter adjustments to improve accuracy.
input: QIIME 2 feature-classifier, classify-sklearn, fit-classifier-naive-bayes, --p-feat-ext--n-features, --p-trunc-len, --p-min-length, --p-max-length, --i-reference-reads
output: The 10,000‑taxa result is not due to a default subset of features; the default setting trains on all reference sequences provided. The --o‑classification file from classify‑sklearn contains classifications for the query sequences you supplied, not a list of all reference taxa. The --p‑feat‑ext‑n‑features parameter refers to the number of k‑mers used during training, not a subset of reference sequences. Default parameter values were chosen to work well for 16S and ITS data; changing them can affect precision and recall. Increasing the confidence threshold will reduce false positives but may increase under‑classification. For large training datasets, multithreading is available only for classification, not for fit‑classifier‑naive‑bayes; training memory can be reduced by lowering the batch size and using fewer jobs. Primer parameters should be supplied in 5′→3′ orientation; the reverse primer is not the reverse complement. Setting --p‑trunc‑len may discard sequences; setting it to zero or adjusting --p‑max‑length can recover more reads. Finally, to evaluate classifier performance, test it on a separate query set or mock community rather than on the reference data used for training.

instruction: Explain why using more reference sequences in the UNITE database results in fewer sample sequences being classified beyond kingdom level (except fungi).
input: QIIME2 2019.4, feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, feature-classifier classify-sklearn, UNITE ver 8 dynamic reference sequences, UNITE dynamic reference taxonomy
output: The k__Fungi;p__unidentified;c__unidentified;o__unidentified;f__unidentified;g__unidentified;s__unidentified classification you are seeing is actually the annotation that is provided in your reference database. The classifier is matching your reads to those reference sequences, so you get assignments that are only at kingdom level. When you train with the full UNITE database, many more sequences have that unidentified annotation, so more reads are matched to them, producing fewer informative assignments. Extracting primer‑specific reads removes many of those ‘junk’ references and gives better performance. If you want to use the full database, filter out the sequences whose taxonomy is all unidentified (e.g., with qiime taxa filter‑seqs or by trimming the FASTA and taxonomy files). Note that newer UNITE releases contain many such sequences, while older releases do not, so the effect depends on the version. Also ensure that sequence IDs match taxonomy IDs (case matters); mismatched IDs can cause the classifier to fail with errors such as "not enough values to unpack". Once the IDs are consistent and the unwanted sequences are removed, the classifier should give more specific assignments.

instruction: Fix the error about importing a FASTA file with invalid characters on line 538 in QIIME2 training of a feature classifier.
input: qiime2-2019.4, qiime tools import, awk, sed, sh_refs_qiime_ver8_dynamic_s_02.02.2019_dev.fasta, line 538
output:
Open the FASTA file and check the symbol at line 538; it must be an IUPAC DNA character. If the line contains leading or trailing whitespace (e.g., a space at the beginning), remove it. One way to do this is to run a command such as 


 sed '538 s/ //g' sh_refs_qiime_ver8_dynamic_s_02.02.2019_dev.fasta > fixed.fasta


or if the line number is different, adjust accordingly (e.g., `sed '67868 s/ //g'`).  If you prefer to edit the file in place, use `sed -i`, but note that this produces no separate output file.  After cleaning the FASTA file, import it again with


 qiime tools import --type 'FeatureData[Sequence]' --input-path fixed.fasta --output-path its-refer.qza


The newer QIIME2 releases are stricter about FASTA format, so removing any stray whitespace or non‑IUPAC characters is usually required.

instruction: Explain how to filter 18S data to retain only protist sequences, determine which taxonomy levels to remove, and explain why fungi appear at D3 level.
input: qiime2 2018.11, silva 132, feature-classifier
output: To keep only protist sequences, filter your feature table by taxonomy. 1) Use a reference database that includes eukaryotes, such as SILVA 138 or PR2. 2) Run the QIIME 2 taxonomy filtering plugin to exclude all non‑protist taxa. 3) When filtering, you can remove higher‑level eukaryote groups that are not protists, e.g., plants and animals, by identifying their taxonomic ranks (e.g., D_4__Animalia, D_4__Phragmoplastophyta). 4) Fungi may appear at the D_3 level because primers are leaky and amplify fungal rRNA genes, so they will be classified as fungi in the taxonomy. 5) After filtering, you will have a table that contains only protist sequences.

instruction: Fix the error encountered when using the feature classifier in QIIME 2 Studio and provide alternative steps if necessary.
input: q2studio, q2cli, feature classifier, VSEARCH-based consensus taxonomy classifier, BLAST+ consensus taxonomy classifier, BAD REQUEST - <class 'qiime2.core.type.grammar.UnionExp'> object has no attribute 'decode'
output: This is a known bug in Q2Studio. You will have to run this particular step with q2cli for right now, though the rest of the analysis can be completed in Q2Studio. There has been some work on this bug, but no satisfactory fix was included in the 2021.8 release, and there will be very little further development on QIIME 2 Studio as focus shifts to q2galaxy.

instruction: Use GTDB release202 files to build a 16S rRNA classifier: identify which taxonomy and sequence files to use, handle multiple gene copies per genome, prepare data, and train a QIIME2 classifier.
input: GTDB release202 taxonomy files ar122_taxonomy_r202.tsv, bac120_taxonomy_r202.tsv, ssu_all_r202.tar.gz, bac120_ssu_reps_r202.tar.gz, ar122_ssu_reps_r202.tar.gz
output:
Download the taxonomy files from GTDB release202: ar122_taxonomy_r202.tsv.gz and bac120_taxonomy_r202.tsv.gz, unzip them. Choose either the representative‑sequence files (bac120_ssu_reps_r202.tar.gz and ar122_ssu_reps_r202.tar.gz) or the full 16S gene collection (ssu_all_r202.tar.gz). For the representative set, extract the tar.gz archives. Import the sequence files into QIIME 2 with: 
qiime tools import 
    --input-path <seq_file>.fna 
    --type 'FeatureData[Sequence]' 
    --output-path <seq_qza>
Import the taxonomy tables with: 
qiime tools import 
    --input-path <tax_file>.tsv 
    --type 'FeatureData[Taxonomy]' 
    --input-format 'HeaderlessTSVTaxonomyFormat' 
    --output-path <tax_qza>
Merge the bacterial and archaeal taxonomies and sequences with: 
qiime feature-table merge-taxa 
    --i-data <bact_tax_qza> <arch_tax_qza> 
    --o-merged-data gtdb_tax.qza
qiime feature-table merge-seqs 
    --i-data <bact_seq_qza> <arch_seq_qza> 
    --o-merged-data gtdb_seqs.qza
Optionally perform quality control with RESCRIPt. Train the naive‑bayes classifier: 
qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes 
    --i-reference-reads gtdb_seqs.qza 
    --i-reference-taxonomy gtdb_tax.qza 
    --o-classifier gtdb_classifier.qza
If using the full 16S collection, download ssu_all_r202.tar.gz, extract the fasta file, parse the fasta headers to create a taxonomy TSV by keeping the first two items (ID and taxonomy) and replacing the space after the ID with a tab. Import that TSV and the fasta file into QIIME 2 as shown above, then train the classifier in the same way. The taxonomy table may be a subset of the sequence IDs; they do not need to match one‑to‑one. GTDB does not provide an NR99 version; that filtering must be done by the user if required.

instruction: Explain why the command 'qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn --i-classifier classifier.qza --i-reads dada2_rep_set.qza --o-classification taxonomy.qza' got killed after running for a while and whether it is due to memory.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, --i-classifier classifier.qza, --i-reads dada2_rep_set.qza, --o-classification taxonomy.qza, Killed
output: The process was terminated by signal 9, indicating it was killed by the system. The /usr/bin/time output shows a maximum resident set size of about 30.5 GB (Maximum resident set size (kbytes): 30402660), which means the job attempted to use more RAM than was available on a machine with 32 GB of RAM and other running processes. This indicates a memory issue. The default values for the options can contribute to high memory usage: --p-n-jobs defaults to 1, and each job can consume a lot of RAM; the --p-reads-per-batch defaults to 20000. You can try running the command without specifying --p-n-jobs, monitor RAM usage with htop, and reduce the value of --p-reads-per-batch to lower memory consumption. If the RAM usage remains too high, you may need to run the analysis on a machine with more memory (e.g., an HPC cluster) or reduce the size of the input data. Increasing the swap space (e.g., to 50 GB) can also help, but the best solution is usually more RAM or smaller data.

instruction: Troubleshoot why the feature-classifier classify-consensus-blast command produces a BLAST6 file empty error with new databases.
input: qiime, feature-classifier, BLAST6 file is empty error
output: Run the command with the --verbose flag to capture detailed output. An empty BLAST6 file typically indicates that no hits were found during the blast search. Verify that your reference reads and taxonomy files are correctly formatted and compatible with the Qiime version you are using. If the command is terminated with a "zsh: killed" message, it may be a memory issue; monitor memory usage during execution and consider allocating more memory if needed.

instruction: Determine why taxonomy hits are lower when analyzing all 384 samples together compared to analyzing 8 samples separately using silva-138 full length classifier in qiime2-amplicon-2023.9 18S analysis.
input: qiime2-amplicon-2023.9, silva-138, dada2-denoising
output: The difference in taxonomy hits can be due to read orientation detection. When many samples are processed together, the orientation detector may be misled by mixed‑orientation reads or a nonsense sequence, causing the classifier to assign taxa incorrectly or leave them unassigned. To resolve this, orient all sequences consistently using qiime rescript orient-seqs, or run classify-sklearn with the --p-read-orientation parameter set to 'same' (instead of the default 'auto'). Using the 'same' orientation parameter fixed the issue and restored proper classification.

instruction: Reclassify the same ASVs using a specified classifier with the read orientation set to either 'auto' or 'same', and investigate why the results differ when using '--p-read-orientation auto'.
input: qiime rescript get-silva-data, qiime rescript cull-seqs, qiime rescript filter-seqs-length-by-taxon, qiime rescript dereplicate, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, usearch, RESCRIPt, QIIME2, --p-read-orientation auto, --p-read-orientation same
output: When the auto‑detect method is used (–p‑read‑orientation "auto") the classifier inspects the first 100 reference sequences to decide whether the reads are 5′‑3′ or 3′‑5′.  If the reference database contains sequences in both orientations, or noisy or reversed sequences, the detector can mis‑identify the orientation.  This mis‑detection leads to poor k‑mer matching and consequently unexpected or erroneous taxonomic assignments.  PacBio CCS reads, which can be longer or fragmentary compared to reference fragments, can also trigger the detector to misfire.  The two SILVA classifiers you compared differ not only in their version (138 vs. 138.1) but also in how they were curated: steps such as cull‑seqs, filter‑seqs‑length‑by‑taxon, dereplicate, and reverse‑transcribe were performed at different times or with different QIIME 2 releases.  These inconsistent curation steps can change the set of reference sequences used for orientation assessment, thereby producing different results when auto orientation is employed.  Setting the orientation explicitly with --p‑read‑orientation "same" (or curating the reference database with RESCRIPt so that all reference sequences share the same orientation) removes the discrepancy and yields consistent taxonomic assignments across classifiers.  For reproducibility and reliability, it is recommended to use the latest SILVA 138.x database and specify the orientation that matches the data (e.g., --p‑read‑orientation same) or to re‑orient the query sequences with RESCRIPt if the data are in mixed orientation.

instruction: Find reasons for and solutions to the 'Killed: 9' error when running q2-feature-classifier classify-sklearn on a dataset of 143 samples using the silva classifier.
input: feature-classifier classify-sklearn, silva classifier, 515/806 pre trained silva classifier, Killed: 9
output: The process is being killed due to out-of-memory usage. Attach detailed error logs or run with --verbose to see more information. Reduce memory usage by setting pre_dispatch to 1 or decreasing chunk_size. If the dataset is large, lower chunk_size further (e.g., 1000 or to the number of sequences). If memory still insufficient, run on a machine with more RAM or use a cloud instance (AWS EC2) or institutional cluster. Note that greengenes works without issue, indicating the problem is specific to the SILVA classifier, which is larger. Reducing the classifier confidence or using a smaller SILVA subset (e.g., 94_otu_fasta) may also help.

instruction: Explain why a QIIME2 ITS classifier is returning only kingdom-level taxonomy and provide troubleshooting steps to achieve deeper taxonomic resolution.
input: qiime2-2017.8, UNITE 99%, feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, ITS primers, truncated reads 250 bp, kingdom-level classification
output: The issue is likely with the classifier or the reference sequences. First, test the classifier on a subset of the same sequences used for training; if it still only returns kingdom, the classifier or taxonomy format is incorrect. Use the QIIME‑formatted UNITE dataset and train the classifier without extracting reference reads, using the developer version of UNITE, which gives the best performance. If problems persist, examine the primer set, sequence length, and sample type, as non‑target sequences can cause low resolution. BLAST the query sequences; if many hits are non‑fungal (e.g., host sequences), remove them. Use filter‑features or exclude‑seqs with a % identity threshold (e.g., 80–90%) to keep only sequences that align to UNITE. Consider using classify‑consensus‑blast with max_accepts set to 10 for a second opinion. If paired‑end reads cannot be merged due to ITS length variability, analyze forward reads alone, which may improve classification depth.

instruction: Explain why the default --p-reads-per-batch value is high and why qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn can take a long time even when using many threads.
input: q2cli, qiime, feature-classifier classify-sklearn, silva-119-99-515-806-nb-classifier.qza, --p-n-jobs 20, --p-reads-per-batch 262144
output: The default --p-reads-per-batch is set very high to effectively turn off batch processing unless the user chooses to use it, because load balancing is the user’s responsibility. With a batch that large only one thread is used, so the --p-n-jobs setting does not give the expected parallelism unless the batch size is reduced (e.g., to 1000). The classify-sklearn command uses a naive Bayes (RDP) classifier that is optimized for accuracy rather than speed; it runs in Java and is not heavily multithreaded. With 40 k query sequences the algorithm is inherently slow, and the size of the SILVA database further increases runtime—SILVA can take up to 30 × longer to classify than Greengenes. The process also consumes a large amount of memory (tens of GB), but on a machine with 1 TB this is not usually the bottleneck. Therefore, the long runtimes are expected for this number of queries and database size, and reducing the batch size or using an alternative classifier such as the fully multithreaded vsearch-based classify-consensus-vsearch may help improve speed.

instruction: Help me understand the meaning of 'feature' in QIIME 2
input: Moving Pictures tutorial, QIIME 2
output: A feature is essentially any unit of observation, e.g., an OTU, a sequence variant, a gene, a metabolite, etc. A feature table is a matrix of sample X feature abundances (the number of times each feature was observed in each sample). We use the generic term "feature" because these features can be many different data types; but if you are familiar with OTUs and/or qiime1, you will probably be more comfortable thinking of features as OTUs and feature tables as OTU tables. In qiime1 the term "OTU table" was used because the only observational unit supported was OTUs. In QIIME2 most features will be OTUs or sequence variants, and we already support multiple observational units (OTUs via clustering methods in q2-vsearch, sequence variants via q2-dada2 and q2-deblur, metagenome features via MetaPhlAn2). Many other types of feature data can be imported into QIIME2 for analysis, and with the proliferation of different technologies and data types, stronger support for these may come in the near future. We adopt the generic term "feature", which is more inclusive than "OTU".

instruction: Change the TMPDIR environment variable to /data so that the classifier does not produce a "No space left on device" error.
input: No space left on device, classifier, export TMPDIR = /data
output:
First check if a directory named /data exists: $ ls /data
If you see an error like "ls: /data: No such file or directory", create the directory: $ mkdir /data
Then export the TMPDIR variable: $ export TMPDIR='/data'
Verify that the variable is set: $ echo $TMPDIR
It should display /data. If it does not, export it again.

instruction: Determine whether it is acceptable to trim sequences from different primer regions to the same overlapping region (515F-806R) and process them together with DEBLUR/DADA2 and classify taxonomy, or process them separately and then combine for taxonomy, and explain the appropriate workflow.
input: DEBLUR, DADA2, taxonomy
output: Use q2‑fragment‑insertion for comparing amplicons from different primers; it handles non‑overlapping regions. If you trim the longer reads to the overlapping 515F‑806R region, you can run DADA2/DEBLUR on the trimmed reads, merge the resulting feature tables and representative sequences, and classify taxonomy on the merged data. Alternatively, process each primer set separately with DADA2/DEBLUR, classify taxonomy separately, then collapse the tables at a chosen taxonomic level (e.g., level 7) and merge them; downstream diversity analyses can then be performed on the merged taxonomy, though this approach reduces ASV resolution. For trimming you can use q2‑cutadapt on the demultiplexed reads before denoising.

instruction: Offer the pretrained V3-V4 region classifier with primers 341F and 805R for others to use and provide download link and command provenance.
input: primers 341F, 805R; Illumina 16S protocol; Greengenes 99_otu_taxonomy.txt; naive bayes; qiime2017.12
output: Hi Bod, I’m interested in the V3‑V4 classifier you used. I have the same primers and would like to see the commands you used to reproduce it. Thank you. Hi @ju4n_dc, here is a Dropbox link to the classifier. Let me know if you run into any problems. You can follow the exact commands within the provenance in the file to see what was exactly done. Hope this helps! – Bod. It worked OK.

instruction: Explain how the machine learning confidence works for the naive Bayes classifier in QIIME2 and describe whether an additional threshold should be set for taxonomy classification.
input: qiime2, naive bayes, feature-classifier classify-sklearn, --p-confidence 0.7
output: Qiime2’s default confidence setting (p‑confidence 0.7) was chosen to balance recall and precision for 16S and ITS data, as described in the referenced preprint. The same confidence threshold is applied at every taxonomic rank; if the classifier is ≥70 % confident in a species‑level assignment, the sequence is assigned at that level; otherwise it reports the deepest rank meeting the threshold. The default is considered cautious to avoid false positives, especially at species level, and is generally appropriate for 16S/ITS data. For other markers there is no published guidance, so using the default is reasonable. No additional threshold is normally needed unless you have a specific precision/recall requirement.

instruction: Determine if there is a method to assign taxonomy to query sequences against the NCBI database using QIIME2, including options like classify-consensus-blast and pre-trained classifiers for the V3-V4 region, and recommend suitable databases and resources.
input: QIIME2, taxonomy assignment, Greengenes classifier, NCBI database, SILVA classifier, V3-V4 region, 16S rRNA, memory error, pre-trained full length SILVA classifier
output: You can use the classify-consensus-blast plugin in QIIME2 to assign taxonomy against an NCBI database. The plugin works with any FASTA sequence database that includes the same taxonomy format as Greengenes, so you can import NCBI sequences and run the method. The default settings search for the top 10 hits with at least 80% identity and assign a consensus taxonomy when more than half of those hits share the same lineage (default minimum consensus 0.51). The full-length SILVA classifier that is pre‑trained in the QIIME2 resource page can be used for V3‑V4 queries, but it may take longer because it searches the entire full‑length alignment. Greengenes and SILVA are recommended reference databases for 16S V3‑V4 analyses. If memory errors occur when training a classifier, consider trimming the full database to the V3‑V4 region (e.g., with qiime feature‑classifier extract-reads) or using a smaller training set.

instruction: Compare the classification output of Nanopore reads from a mock community using qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-blast and qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-vsearch, investigate why the unassigned rates differ (9% vs 56%), and discuss exposing the -num_threads parameter for Blast in a future release.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-blast, qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-vsearch, --i-query rep-seqs.qza, --i-reference-reads $DB, --i-reference-taxonomy $TAXONOMY, --p-perc-identity 0.77, --p-query-cov 0.3, --p-maxaccepts 1, --o-classification taxonomy.qza, --p-top-hits-only, --p-strand 'both', --p-unassignable-label 'Unassigned', --p-threads $THREADS, 9% vs 56% unassigned rates
output:
VSEARCH may report two hits when strand='both'; if those hits have different taxonomies (or taxonomy is not multi‑level), the query is marked "Unassigned". The maxhits parameter (to be added in a future release) will allow control of how many top hits are used for consensus. In the meantime, running VSEARCH directly can help diagnose the issue.

The difference in unassigned rates is largely due to this behavior rather than the 30% query coverage setting. When maxhits is set to 1, consensus taxonomy assignment is not performed because only a single hit is kept. maxaccepts controls how many sequences are accepted before stopping the search; maxhits then selects the top N of those accepted.

Regarding BLAST, it does not currently expose a separate maxhits parameter and maxaccepts determines how many hits are returned. Multi‑threading for classify‑consensus‑blast (the -num_threads option) is an open issue and may be added in a future release.

instruction: Explain how to reduce memory usage when training a classifier with qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes on the silver 99%132 database.
input: qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, --p-classify--chunk-size, --p-feat-ext--n-features, MemoryError
output: Adjust the --p-classify--chunk-size parameter when fitting the classifier; avoid changing --p-feature-ext--n-features because it reduces the number of k-mers considered and may affect classifier performance. If you have limited memory, modifying chunk-size is sufficient.

instruction: Resolve the 'Unable to allocate array with shape (74619, 8192) and data type float64' error when training a QIIME2 classifier with SILVA data on a system with 32GB RAM.
input: qiime2, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, error: Unable to allocate array with shape (74619, 8192) and data type float64
output: The error is an out‑of‑memory error. SILVA is large and often needs more than 32 GB of RAM. Use a machine with more memory or a compute cluster. As a workaround, reduce the batch size with the --p-reads-per-batch parameter (e.g. 1000 or 2000).

instruction: Choose the appropriate SILVA database version for 16S metagenomic analysis of environmental samples, specifically whether to use Ref NR 99 or Ref from the SILVA 138.1 download options.

instruction: Explain how to handle ASVs that lack taxonomic annotation when collapsing a 16S ASV table to a genus level.
input: q2-feature-classifier classify-sklearn, GreenGenes2 2024.09, ASV table, 16S V3-V4
output: Do not discard ASVs that lack genus annotation; they are valid sequences. Keep them as separate ASVs or give them a generic unknown genus label (e.g., g__unknown_1). When collapsing for visualization, accept that the best collapse is to the lowest resolved taxonomic level (family here) and leave lower levels blank or as unknown. If detailed information is needed, analyze at the ASV level.

instruction: I have paired-end sequencing reads and used the QIIME 2 pipeline to identify bacterial species, but after denoising I only see 40 sequence counts and the taxonomy analysis only assigns the top-level taxon k-Bacteria; guide me on determining which bacterial species are present in the sample.

output: The poor taxonomic assignments suggest the reads may be contaminant or off-target. Manual BLAST searches, even after ignoring uncultured/unclassified reads, also return poor assignments.

instruction: Explain how a query sequence with Ns padded within or at its beginning would interact with database entries in blast, vsearch, and sklearn, and discuss effects of concatenated reads on each tool.
input: blast, vsearch, sklearn, QIIME 2, Dada2
output: The tools treat Ns literally. The recommended practice is to remove ambiguous bases before classification, because they increase uncertainty. Most QC and denoising steps already eliminate reads with Ns, and reference databases also filter them. The sklearn classifier assumes that ambiguous bases have been removed upstream; a single or few Ns may slightly pollute the k‑mer profile but usually have negligible effect; long stretches of Ns can cause misclassification if the reference also contains Ns. For concatenated paired‑end reads with an N spacer, global aligners such as VSEARCH can handle them, but the sklearn classifier cannot, and concatenation is discouraged in QIIME 2 because it can disrupt alignments and k‑mer profiles used for taxonomy. VSEARCH supports a concatenation‑with‑N‑spacer option, but other downstream tools may misbehave, so it is best to avoid passing such sequences to them.

instruction: Help troubleshoot why QIIME 2 feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes returns "not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 0)" after extracting reference reads and using reference taxonomy.
input: qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, --i-sequences 2022.10.seqs.fna.qza, --p-f-primer ACTCCTACG..., --p-r-primer GGACTACHV..., --p-trunc-len 446, --p-min-length 100, --p-max-length 450, --i-reference-reads reference-seqs.qza, --i-reference-taxonomy ref-tax.qza, --o-classifier classifier.qza, --verbose, Plugin error from feature-classifier: not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 0).

instruction: Explain how to correctly cite the trained SILVA database, whether a trained SILVA 138.2 full‑length classifier compatible with the latest QIIME2 release exists, and address the use of different 16S rRNA regions.
input: QIIME2, SILVA 138.2
output: To cite the trained SILVA database, reference the SILVA v138.2 database and the QIIME2 plugin used, and include the specific rRNA region. A full‑length trained classifier for SILVA 138.2 is not currently available; the classifiers on the QIIME2 Resources page are region‑specific and were trained before the release of SILVA 138.2. If a full‑length classifier is needed, it can be trained separately. The taxonomy names in the older 138.1 classifiers are outdated; SILVA 138.2 updates these names. Species‑level assignments from SILVA are generally unreliable because the database curates only down to genus level, and the species labels added via the RESCRIPt plugin are not validated. Therefore species‑level assignments from 16S amplicons, even with full‑length data, should be treated with caution.

instruction: Resolve the inconsistent taxonomic assignments of Mycobacterium sequences in Qiime2 2024.10 using Silva 138.2 classifier.

output: Species‑level assignments from SILVA are unreliable. Work at the genus level or use ASV sequences. If species identification is required, confirm with another technique such as qPCR or targeted amplification. The old D_x__ schema is outdated; use RESCRIPt to build a newer SILVA database that provides Greengenes‑style taxonomy strings for more accurate classification.

instruction: Explain whether a publicly available pre‑trained full‑length 16S classifier .qza file exists, and if not, recommend strategies to optimize or speed up training in environments with abundant RAM, including any user examples or workarounds.
input: QIIME 2, classify-classifier-naive-bayes, Metasquare, .qza
output: The answer suggests removing redundant and low‑quality sequences before training, using RESCRIPt dereplication and cull steps, creating an amplicon‑specific classifier by extracting the region, dereplicating, removing low‑quality sequences and then training, and possibly increasing the --p-classify--chunk-size. It also notes that a pre‑trained full‑length 16S classifier can be downloaded from the QIIME 2 Library (SILVA, Greengenes2, GTDB). If training a custom classifier is still desired, it recommends using VSEARCH for taxonomy assignment (qiime feature‑classifier classify‑consensus‑vsearch) so no separate classifier .qza is needed, and highlights that VSEARCH can be slower than classify‑sklearn for large databases but may be faster for classification after training. The answer stresses that training can take a long time, that curating the reference database (reducing redundancy, avoiding bias) helps, and that training once and re‑using the classifier is efficient. Users have found VSEARCH and RESCRIPt useful, and some have switched to the SILVA database for speed, with many processors sometimes helping but still consuming large RAM.

instruction: Explain how classify-consensus-vsearch determines consensus taxonomy for a given ASV and whether truncation occurs when reference hits lack genus-level annotations.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-vsearch, --p-perc-identity 0.97, --p-maxaccepts 5, --p-min-consensus 0.51, ASV
output: For an ASV, classify-consensus-vsearch looks at the top hits sorted by identity. It checks each taxonomic rank to see if the proportion of hits that contain the same label at that rank is at least the minimum consensus threshold (51 % by default). In the example, the genus label g__Creontiades appears in only 2 of the 5 top hits, so it does not reach the 51 % threshold. No rank meets the threshold, so the algorithm backs up one level. At the family level all hits contain f__Miridae, so that label is chosen as the consensus taxonomy: k__Metazoa;p__Arthropoda;c__Insecta;o__Hemiptera;f__Miridae. The truncation at the family level happens because one of the reference hits lacks a genus annotation; missing annotations are treated as absent, preventing consensus at lower ranks. To avoid this, always include all rank levels in the reference taxonomy, even as empty placeholders (e.g., g__ , s_). RESCRIPt actions such as get_silva_data or get-ncbi-data can add these placeholders with the --p-rank-propagation flag, and edit-taxonomy can insert missing prefixes.

instruction: Train a V3/V4 classifier using SILVA 138 in qiime2.2024.10 and investigate why no output is generated.
output: The most common reason for no output is a lack of RAM. The job can finish silently if memory is exhausted. Check that you are looking in the correct directory; you can use !pwd and !ls in a Jupyter cell to verify the path. Running the command directly from the shell instead of Jupyter may reveal error messages. If you only have 32 GB of RAM, try a machine with 64 GB; this has produced an output for similar setups.

instruction: Use the trimmed BEExact references for the V3–V4 region with QIIME 2 to train a classifier and classify 16S sequences, ensuring correct settings for confidence, orientation, and trimming, and verify the results.
input: qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, p-f-primer, p-r-primer, p-min-length, p-max-length, BEEx_FL-refs_sequences.qza, BEEx-V3V4-refs_sequences.qza, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, --p-confidence
output:
First, confirm the confidence threshold: the BEExact tutorial sets --p-confidence to 0.5, while the default is 0.7; try using 0.5 and observe if classification improves.
Second, test the trimmed references with alternative classifiers such as Greengenes, SILVA, or RDP to see if poor classifications persist; if they do, the issue may lie with the data itself.
Third, address potential mixed-orientation reads by reorienting the sequences with RESCRIPt tools (qiime rescript orient-reads or orient-seqs) before running the classifier.
Fourth, inspect the trimmed reference file with qiime feature-table tabulate-seqs and compare it to your query sequences; verify that the trimmed region matches what was actually sequenced.
Follow these steps to diagnose why the trimmed references collapse into one phylum.

instruction: Assess whether building Snakemake pipelines that download GTDB, extract variable regions, dereplicate sequences, and train naive Bayes classifiers for QIIME 2 releases would be useful, and suggest ways to streamline and upload them to RESCRIPt.
input: Snakemake, GTDB, V4, V3V4, naive Bayes classifier, QIIME 2, RESCRIPt
output: Collaborators are interested in building pipelines, and are working on similar projects using Nextflow and RESCRIPt. They suggest collaborating on a RESCRIPt pipeline to replace current classifier training workflows. They plan to create Snakemake pipelines for common databases such as SILVA, UNITE, GTDB, and more complex pipelines that pull from GenBank and extract sequence segments. A separate GitHub repository is recommended for these pipelines, with a modular structure. Results can be shared via GitHub releases or an official QIIME 2 documentation location. Hardware options include institutional HPC and cloud-native environments. The discussion includes considerations about packaging, clean-up, generalizing outputs, structuring filenames and wildcards, and the advantages of using Snakemake versus custom scripts, including reproducibility with conda environments and job submissions to SLURM or similar schedulers.

instruction: Explain whether it is acceptable to modify taxonomy files after classification and describe the correct method to combine taxonomies for fungal ITS amplicon data.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-blast, R, Unite database
output: Use the RESCRIPt plugin's merge-taxa action to combine taxonomies. Avoid the lca mode; consider the length mode so you keep the longest taxonomy classification. Inspect BLAST results to confirm hits are reasonable and check for unexpected clades. Non‑target ITS sequences can appear; use NCBI BLAST to spot‑check a few and filter them out if necessary. Mixed sequence orientation can also cause classification problems; classify-sklearn assumes all sequences are in the same orientation, whereas classify-consensus-* can handle both. Use the REScrip orient‑seqs action to orient your representative sequences, then reclassify and merge taxonomies.

instruction: Provide a trained classifier.qza file for the SILVA 132.2SSU ref NR 99 dataset for 16S RNA, or suggest alternatives if the dataset is too large.

instruction: Decide whether to use a --p-trunc-len of 326 when training a classifier on 454 single-end reads that have been truncated to 326 bp, given that they are functionally equivalent to merged paired-end reads.
input: cutadapt, DADA2, qiime2, qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, RESCRIPt, truncating at 326, min 250, max 600, --p-trunc-len
output: If the 454 reads have been trimmed to the full amplicon between primer pairs and you are confident that all sequences are present, you do not need to truncate the reference database to 326 bp. Truncating may introduce taxonomic bias, especially when mixing single-end and paired-end data. If there is uncertainty that the reads contain the entire region, truncating the reference to 326 bp can be useful. Many analysts choose not to truncate so that classifiers are consistent across different dataset types.

instruction: Provide an updated or alternative link to a pre‑trained UNITE classifier for ITS1 that is compatible with QIIME 2 version 2021.4 or 2024.2, and explain how to access it.
input: Git LFS pointer file, "Git LFS is disabled for this repository" error, GitHub repository, data.qiime2.org 404
output: You can obtain a ready‑to‑use ITS classifier from the GitHub repository (the file is tracked with Git LFS, so install Git LFS before cloning or downloading). The README contains instructions. Additionally, the GitHub repo "colinbrislawn/unite‑train" hosts ITS classifiers for the UNITE database that work with QIIME 2 2021.4 and 2024.2. Installing Git LFS allows you to download the classifier from this repo. Older QIIME 2 versions are still supported, but it is recommended to use the most recent release.

instruction: Determine whether it is possible to train a new classifier using the provided Greengenes2 sequence and taxonomy files, and decide whether to create separate classifiers for different piglet age groups or use a single classifier for all age groups.
input: 2024.09.seqs.fna.qza, 2024.09.taxonomy.id.tsv.qza
output: It is possible to train a new classifier with your own sequences and taxonomy files, but this approach is not recommended because it limits the database to the scope of the previously annotated community. A single classifier is preferable to separate classifiers for each age group. Use RESCRIPt tutorials (adapted for predownloaded GG2 sequences) to train the classifier, or consult the GG2 repository for additional guidance.

instruction: Attempt to classify 16S sequences to species level using GTDB classifier in QIIME and investigate why species-level assignments are missing.
input: qiime tools import, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, qiime metadata tabulate, gtdb-214-both-classifier.qza
output: Likely outside your control. Assuming you are trying to classify short / non-full-length sequences, this is a common issue. In fact, being unable to disambiguate genera and species from one another, even using full length 16S rRNA gene sequences, is not uncommon. RDP has not been discontinued, but is now hosted elsewhere. It is at least available in source-forge. In fact, you can use the RESCRIPt plugin to fetch the latest version of RDP following this tutorial. You can also follow our SSU tutorial for GenBank. Assuming the rank designations are similar, and they do not differ too much with their taxonomic schema, you should be able to merge the taxonomy and sequence files together, then make a classifier. I've not tried this before, so I can't comment on how well it'd work. The closest thing I can currently think of, you can try is the feature-classifier classify-consensus-vsearch with the --p-top-hits-only flag. I am sure someone else might be able to provide a better or more creative solution. You can also consider using the weighted classifiers constructed through q2-clawback.

instruction: Explain how to download all available SSU (16S) sequences for all species from GTDB using QIIME2 RESCRIPt.
input: qiime rescript get-gtdb-data --p-version 214 --p-domain Both
output:
To download all available SSU (16S) sequences from GTDB, you can follow these steps:

1. Download and unzip the file:
   wget https://data.gtdb.ecogenomic.org/releases/release214/214.1/genomic_files_all/ssu_all_r214.tar.gz
   unzip ssu_all_r214.tar.gz

2. Extract taxonomy from the FASTA headers:
   egrep '^>' ssu_all_r214.fna | tr -d '>' | sed 's/ d__/\t d__/' | sed 's/\[.*//' > ssu_all_r214_taxonomy.tsv

3. Import taxonomy into QIIME 2:
   qiime tools import --input-path ssu_all_r214_taxonomy.tsv --input-format HeaderlessTSVTaxonomyFormat --type 'FeatureData[Taxonomy]' --output-path ssu-all-r214-taxonomy.qza

4. Import sequences into QIIME 2:
   qiime tools import --input-path ssu_all_r214.fna --input-format DNAFASTAFormat --type 'FeatureData[Sequence]' --output-path ssu-all-r214-sequence.qza

5. Optionally perform curation with RESCRIPt:
   qiime rescript …

6. Train a classifier:
   qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes --i-reference-reads ssu-all-r214-sequence.qza --i-reference-taxonomy ssu-all-r214-taxonomy.qza --o-classifier ssu-all-r214-classifier.qza

instruction: Fix the scikit-learn version mismatch errors that prevent training and using the GTDB feature classifier in a QIIME 2024.2 conda environment after a recent OS update.
input: QIIME 2024.2, scikit-learn, get-gtdb-data, fit-classifier-naive-bayes, classify-sklearn, q2_feature_classifier
output: Both problems stem from the scikit-learn version installed. The environment has scikit-learn 1.3.2, while the amplicon distribution expects 0.24.1. The newer scikit-learn likely came from a pip install or an OS change. Recreate a fresh qiime2-amplicon-2024.2 environment, which restores the correct scikit-learn version and resolves the errors.

instruction: Explain why an older GTDB classifier gives higher species‑level resolution than newer versions, assess whether database growth reduces confidence in species assignment, and recommend which classifier (latest GTDB or Greengenes 2) to prioritize for full‑length 16S data, considering assignment performance at kingdom to genus levels after filtering.
input: dada2, GTDB 4.2, GTDB 4.5, Greengenes 2
output: Older GTDB versions give higher species‑level resolution because larger database size can reduce confidence in species assignment (paper: database size positively correlates with loss of species‑level resolution). You can compare results by using the same database version but different implementations (full SSU vs species representatives). The latest GTDB (or Greengenes 2) is recommended over older GTDB because of updated taxonomy. Using assignTaxonomy with tryRC and a minimum bootstrap of 80 improves assignment confidence; Greengenes 2 provides more reads assigned and higher bootstrap confidence than GTDB in this dataset.

instruction: Use SILVA to assign taxonomy in QIIME2 without the pre-trained classifier and the classify-sklearn command.
input: classify-sklearn, --p-pre-dispatch 1, --p-reads-per-batch 500
output: Use the pre‑trained full‑length SILVA classifier that is available on the QIIME 2 data resources page instead of training a new one. Make sure you train or use a classifier for a single marker (16S or 18S only) rather than a combined 16S+18S database to reduce memory usage. If the dataset is small, you can also use OTU‑pre‑clustered sequences (e.g., 99 % or 97 % clusters) to further lower the memory requirement. If RAM is limited, consider using the smaller Greengenes pre‑trained classifiers or the BLAST or VSEARCH consensus classifiers in q2‑feature‑classifier, which are less memory‑intensive (though slightly less accurate). Finally, if you are running QIIME 2 in a virtual machine, increase the RAM and CPU allocation using the VirtualBox guide; the default 1 GB is usually insufficient for taxonomic classification.

instruction: Run QIIME2 classify-sklearn with the pre‑trained Silva classifier and, if a Memory Error occurs, determine if the error is caused by the size of the Silva file and fix it by allocating more RAM, adjusting memory‑intensive options, or using a lighter classifier.
input: QIIME2 2017.11, classify-sklearn, classify-consensus-blast, classify-consensus-vsearch, --p-reads-per-batch, --p-chunk-size, --p-n-jobs, 3GB RAM, 4GB RAM, 12GB RAM
output:
Silva classifiers use up to 11 GB memory. With only 3–12 GB available the command fails. To fix the error you can:
- Allocate more RAM to the virtual machine.
- Run the command in single‑job mode (omit or set --p-n-jobs to 1) instead of parallel.
- Reduce the reads‑per‑batch (e.g., --p-reads-per-batch 1000 or 500).
- Avoid parallel classification, which loads multiple copies of the classifier.
If these steps still fail, use a less memory‑intensive classifier such as the pre‑trained Greengenes, or try classify‑consensus‑blast or classify‑consensus‑vsearch instead of classify‑sklearn.

instruction: Determine whether the SILVA 128 QIIME compatible database includes large subunit ribosomal RNA reference sequences or only small subunit sequences.

instruction: What is the equivalent of the biom file that contains OTU ID, consensus lineage, and sample ID, in QIIME2?

output: In QIIME2 the equivalent is a feature table artifact, such as FeatureTable[Frequency]. Taxonomy information is not stored in the feature table but in a separate feature metadata artifact.

instruction: Describe how to use QIIME to identify unclassified OTUs, filter them, and perform BLAST searches, including how to view results and whether the QIIME pipeline needs to be rerun after BLASTing.
input: QIIME, fastq, BLAST
output: Use the QIIME rep‑seqs visualization artifact: the feature rows are hyperlinked to NCBI BLAST, so you can click to BLAST unclassified reads directly. If you have many unclassified reads, first filter them with qiime taxa filter‑seqs (e.g., --p-include Unclassified) to create a filtered‑seqs.qza file, then use qiime feature‑table tabulate‑seqs to make a filtered‑rep‑seqs.qzv file. The tabulate‑seqs output shows only the unclassified sequences and contains the BLAST hyperlinks, so you don’t need to copy/paste each sequence into NCBI BLAST. Spot‑check the BLAST results to see if the sequences match host DNA (e.g., human, mouse, plant) or if they have low‑quality matches indicating errors. You do not need to rerun the entire QIIME pipeline on the BLAST outputs; instead, use the filtered sequences to decide whether to remove or reclassify them in your analysis.

instruction: Replace ASV IDs with SILVA taxonomy IDs by mapping or clustering.
input: ASV IDs, SILVA taxonomy IDs
output: Direct one‑to‑one conversion is not possible because ASVs and SILVA IDs are not synonymous. One way to map them is to obtain SILVA IDs from the taxonomy file (for example, /SILVA_132_QIIME_release/taxonomy/16S_only/99/taxonomy_7_levels.txt), then use Python to unpack your biom table, replace the hash IDs with the SILVA IDs, and ensure uniqueness by adding a hash or partial hash if duplicates occur. After updating the table you must also modify the taxonomy.qza and rep-seqs.qza files so that the names match across all files. An alternative approach is to perform closed‑reference OTU clustering (e.g., using q2‑vsearch) which produces a table of SILVA OTU IDs instead of ASV IDs.

instruction: Use QIIME 2 to train a SILVA 16SV3-V4 classifier with the provided reference reads and taxonomy, but the training command fails with a segmentation fault. Resolve the issue.
input: qiime, feature-classifier, fit-classifier-naive-bayes, ref-seqs-v34.qza, 99_taxonomy.qza, silva132_v34_classifier.qza, segmentation fault, core dumped, qiime2-2019.7
output: Segmentation faults during QIIME 2 classifier training are usually caused by memory or disk‑space limits. First, clear any temporary files to free disk space and ensure that the system has enough RAM. On 32‑bit systems the address space is limited, so verify that the OS is 64‑bit; a 32‑bit machine will run out of address space and fail to allocate large arrays. Even on a 64‑bit machine 32 GB of RAM may be insufficient if background processes are using memory; try killing unnecessary processes or use a more powerful machine. If you cannot obtain sufficient resources, you can use the pre‑trained SILVA classifier provided by QIIME 2, which usually gives results that are nearly identical to a locally trained classifier.

instruction: Check if there are plans to update Silva138 database for Qiime.
input: Silva138 database, Qiime

instruction: Determine whether a 32GB memory system can resolve the classify-sklearn memory error or if alternative classifiers for 18S sequences are needed.
input: classify-sklearn, 32 GB memory, Silva memory error
output: Use a less memory-intensive classifier such as greengenes. Try newer SILVA 138 classifiers, which have a smaller memory footprint—especially versions without species labels. Reducing the reads-per-batch parameter also lowers memory demands.

instruction: Determine how to resolve the lack of overlap between the SEPP tree and a Rescript-generated database for fragment insertion, and suggest ways to make the process easier.
input: qiime, rescript, silva, sepp
output: SEPP does not alter sequence IDs; the mismatch is caused by differences in filtering, clustering and database versions.  A partial overlap is expected.  Possible solutions that were suggested include: 1) filter the Rescript database to keep only the IDs that appear in the SEPP tree, use fragment‑insertion to insert the missing sequences and then prune the tree; 2) build a new reference tree from the Rescript sequences (e.g., align and construct a tree with IQ‑TREE) and compare it to the SEPP tree; 3) use the pre‑built QIIME‑compatible SEPP tree, which already matches the reference database.  Greater transparency between the three datasets would help, and creating a shared resource that contains the sequences, taxonomy and tree may reduce the need for each user to rebuild the reference themselves.

instruction: Explain why the command is killed during the qiime2 feature-classifier classify-consensus-vsearch step with silva 138 and suggest how to resolve it.
input: qiime2, qiime feature-classifier classify-consensus-vsearch, vsearch, silva-138, threads=18, RAM=62.6GB, cores=24
output: The classification step loads the Silva database (~6GB RAM per instance). With 18 threads, RAM may be insufficient, causing the process to be killed. Try reducing threads to 10, or 1‑4 threads to lower RAM usage. In your case, switching to qiime 2022.2 and using 10 threads resolved the issue. The problem seems specific to 2022.8 but is not fully understood.

instruction: Explain why kraken2 and silva identified Prevotellaceae at family level while greengenes did not, why greengenes classified Prevotella under Bacteroidaceae, and whether this indicates an error.
input: kraken2, greengenes, silva, qiime2, classify-sklearn, gg-2022-10-nb-classifier.qza, silva-138-99-nb-classifier.qza
output: The differences arise because each reference database follows its own nomenclatural rules and updates at different paces. Greengenes2 derives its taxonomy from GTDB, which has moved Prevotella into the family Bacteroidaceae; therefore Greengenes classifies Prevotella under that family. Kraken2 and SILVA use databases that still place Prevotella in Prevotellaceae, so they report that family. NCBI taxonomy is not an authoritative source for nomenclature, so its placement may differ from GTDB. Thus the observed discrepancies are expected and do not necessarily indicate an error in your analysis.

instruction: Determine whether there is an updated Greengenes database for training feature classifiers and whether the 85-OUT_taxonomy.txt file is useful for any database or only for the tutorial example.
input: Greengenes 85_OTU.fasta, 16S V3-V4, tutorial, 85-OUT_taxonomy.txt
output: The 85% OTUs file used in the feature‑classifier tutorial comes from the Greengenes 13_8 release (August 2013), which is the latest available Greengenes release. The pre‑trained classifiers on the data resources page are also trained with Greengenes 13_8. If you need a more up‑to‑date database, you can train with the SILVA database or use one of the pre‑trained SILVA classifiers that QIIME 2 distributes; SILVA has regular updates and links to databases that can be imported into QIIME 2 and to pre‑trained classifiers. The 85-OUT_taxonomy.txt file is part of the Greengenes 13_8 reference database. The tutorial uses the 85% OTUs to keep the example fast, but for real analyses you would normally train a classifier on the 99% OTUs, which are also available in Greengenes 13_8, and pre‑trained classifiers for the 99% OTUs are provided. The taxonomy files such as 85_otu_taxonomy.txt and 99_otu_taxonomy.txt are included in the Greengenes reference database that is linked on the data resources page.

instruction: Explain the differences between Greengenes99%, SILVA123, and SILVA128 databases, whether they provide species-level information, and give guidance on choosing between them.
input: classifier(Greengenes99%OTS), ref.seqs.qza
output: Greengenes is a 16S/Archaea database, while SILVA covers 16S, 18S, and Archaea (and also has 23S/28S, though it is unclear if those are in a QIIME‑compatible format). SILVA 123 and 128 are simply successive versions of the SILVA database. Both Greengenes and SILVA contain reference taxonomies that include species‑level annotations, but whether you obtain species‑level classifications for your data depends on the feature‑classification algorithm and its parameter settings. There is no absolute answer for which reference database to use; each has strengths and limitations. Factors to consider include the marker gene you are targeting (e.g., 18S data cannot use Greengenes), the size of the reference database (SILVA is larger and may require more CPU time and memory), the frequency of database updates (SILVA is regularly updated while Greengenes’ last release was in August 2013), and the methods used to construct the database. It is advisable to consult the official Greengenes and SILVA websites and associated publications, and to try both databases and compare your results.

instruction: Create a Naive Bayes classifier for the V1V2 region of 16S using the SILVA 128 database and determine whether the SILVA 119 99% OTU full‑length classifier is equivalent to the one you are trying to create.
input: qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, killed
output: The "killed" message is usually caused by insufficient RAM when training the classifier. The SILVA database is large, and many laptops cannot handle the memory requirements. Try running the command on a machine with more memory or on a cluster. If you cannot access sufficient memory, you can use the pre‑trained SILVA 119 full‑length Naive Bayes classifier, which is close enough for most purposes; it is not the same as a classifier trained on SILVA 128, but it will work.

instruction: Explain why the SILVA database was used instead of Greengenes in the classifier, given that Greengenes has not been updated in five years.
input: QIIME2
output: Pre-trained classifiers are available for both SILVA and Greengenes on the QIIME2 resources page. In the tutorials, Greengenes classifiers are used because they are smaller and faster for quick examples. In practice, QIIME users have many different priorities and opinions on which reference database is right for them. I’m glad you’re enjoying QIIME2!

instruction: Explain why using the downloaded SILVA classifier file directly with qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn fails, and how to fix.
input: qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, silva-132-99-515-806-nb-classifier.qza, rep-seqs.qza

Reducing the number of jobs (p-n-jobs) won't help; try lowering the reads-per-batch (e.g., 2000) or use Greengenes instead of SILVA, which uses less memory. instruction: Create the QIIME 2 artifact files needed to run qiime diversity core-metrics-phylogenetic using your OTU table (.txt), metadata (.tsv), and unaligned FASTA (.fa).
input: qiime, dada2, qiime diversity core-metrics-phylogenetic, OTU table .txt, metadata .tsv, unaligned FASTA .fa
output:
Convert your OTU table to BIOM format with the command:

biom convert \
  --i-input-file otu_table.txt \
  --o-output-pfile otu_table.biom \
  --to-hdf5 \
  --table-type otu_table

If you have taxonomy data, add the flag --process-obs-metadata. After converting, import the table and sequences into QIIME 2 using the Data Import tutorial, especially the feature tables section. Use your unaligned FASTA file for building a phylogenetic tree, as recommended. Then you can proceed with qiime diversity core-metrics-phylogenetic.

instruction: Explain why consensus_taxonomy_7_levels and consensus_taxonomy_all_levels give different results for 18S dataset using SILVA 132 reference, particularly at the 3rd rank Archaeplastida, and recommend which file to use for eukaryotic data.
input: SILVA 132, consensus_taxonomy_7_levels, consensus_taxonomy_all_levels, Qiime 2, 18S dataset, reference-taxonomy files
output: The differences are caused by the 'all levels' file confusing classifiers that expect uniform taxonomic ranks, so the 3rd level is not consistently class. Therefore, the 7‑level taxonomy, which has uniform ranks, is recommended for eukaryotic data.

instruction: Explain why the SILVA database uses uracil instead of thymine in its fasta sequences, whether 16S software like vsearch recognizes U as T, whether SILVA provides a 16S-only database, the presence of 18S sequences, and when to use the align database versus a regular fasta database, including advantages or disadvantages.
input: greengenes,SILVA_138_SSURef_NR99_tax_silva.fasta,vsearch
output: The SILVA alignment uses uracil (U) rather than thymine (T) because the curated alignment is guided by rRNA secondary structure, which reflects the true RNA sequence and helps reduce alignment ambiguity. The U can be replaced with T if needed for downstream tools. SILVA contains both 16S and 18S rRNA sequences; they are homologous, so keeping them together in the same alignment is valid and helps identify and remove off‑target 18S reads that might be amplified with 16S primers. The align database is a secondary structure‑informed alignment that can produce more robust alignments for phylogenetic inference; it is especially useful when alignment ambiguity is a concern. However, many standard tools such as MAFFT can perform well without secondary‑structure information, so the choice depends on the user’s needs and experience.

instruction: Create a reference tree for fragment insertion using the SILVA v138 database, ensuring the tree is built from full-length reference sequences rather than just the targeted amplicon region.
input: SILVA v138
output: Yes, you can build a SILVA 138 reference tree by following the documented steps. Export the aligned FASTA for the high‑quality sequences you want to use, trim them to the relevant 16S region if necessary, and export the corresponding tree. Then clean up the tree: resolve any polytomies and re‑estimate branch lengths. The reference tree should be calculated using the full‑length reference sequences, which provides a more reliable phylogeny. These steps are similar to those used for older SILVA versions but adapted for v138.

instruction: Identify the files in the SILVA 132 QIIME-compatible database that can be used for taxonomic classification of 16S rRNA ASVs in QIIME 2 and explain how those files were constructed from the raw SILVA data.
input: Silva_132_release.zip, SILVA_132_QIIME_release, rep_set, taxonomy, .fna files, clustering 99%,97%,94%,90%
output: The database contains a rep_set/rep_set_16S_only directory and a taxonomy/16S_only directory.  All reference sequences, including eukaryotic 18S, are recommended to be kept because they allow identification of off‑target sequences.  Each of the four rep_set subdirectories contains a single file (silva_132_90_16S.fna, silva_132_94_16S.fna, silva_132_97_16S.fna, silva_132_99_16S.fna).  These .fna files are lists of unique representative sequences that were obtained by clustering the processed SILVA sequences at the specified percent identities (99, 97, 94, 90).  The construction of these files follows the steps described in the notes: full 16S and 18S rRNA sequences are downloaded from SILVA, all U characters are converted to T and gaps are removed, sequences are dereplicated, sorted by length, clustered at the chosen identities, and then split by domain.  The 99% OTU set (silva_132_99_16S.fna) is commonly used for training classifiers because it removes some extra noise and reduces the size of the data set, and it is appropriate for classifying ASVs derived from DADA2 output.  The taxonomy strings are removed from the sequence files to reduce file size; the taxonomy information is provided separately in the taxonomy directory.  Representative OTU sequence names are derived from the raw data, and the consensus or majority taxonomy for each OTU is generated by considering all lineages that fall into that OTU and collapsing the taxonomy until a consensus or majority is achieved.  For users who wish to create their own curated reference database, the RESCRIPt tool can be used, e.g. by running `qiime rescript get-silva-data --p-version 132 --p-target SSURef_NR99 --p-include-species-labels --output-dir silva-132` and then following the SILVA tutorial.

instruction: Decide whether to download SILVA version 138 formatted with RESCRIPt or version 132 from the SILVA website for processing 18S data with QIIME.
input: SILVA, 18S, QIIME, RESCRIPTr
