File choice depends on the tool: classifier files are for feature‑classifier classify‑sklearn, while sequence and taxonomy files are for feature‑classifier classify‑consensus‑vsearch or classify‑consensus‑blast.

instruction: Explain the meaning of 'plant-corpus' and its suitability for conifer needles samples.
output: These terms come from the Earth Microbiome Project Ontology (EMPO), so you can see there for specifics on all EMPO classifications. Plant corpus is described as 'tissue of leaf, stem, fruit, algae', so yes this sounds suitable for conifer needle samples. However, plant corpus is one of the least common classifications in the EMP data. You might also want to try 'Plant Surface', which might be suitable for conifer needles.

instruction: Determine whether the high abundance of Cyanobacteria assignments (chloroplasts) in mucosal scrapings is due to chloroplasts or mitochondria, and explain how to confirm this.
input: SILVA
output: Take the suspect sequences and submit them to the SILVA‑ACT (Alignment, Classification and Tree Service). Select the appropriate options to obtain an alignment and tree showing how your sequences relate to known references. You need at least four sequences for this to work. If the sequences are chloroplasts, they will group with other chloroplast (plant) sequences and be excluded from bacterial or cyanobacterial groups. To confirm this, download well‑annotated chloroplast 16S rRNA sequences and cyanobacterial sequences from GenBank or SILVA, place them together with your ASVs, construct an alignment and phylogeny, and observe where your sequences fall. Alternatively, BLAST the suspect sequences manually through GenBank or via the QIIME visualization tool.

instruction: Explain the differences between classify-consensus-vsearch and BLAST-based percent identity thresholds, and whether a .7 --p-perc-identity is sufficient for confident species-level assignments.
input: classify-consensus-vsearch, BLAST, q2-feature-classifier, --p-perc-identity, maxhits, top-hits-only, PR2, SILVA
output: Percent‑identity thresholds used with BLAST (e.g. >99% → species, 97% → genus) are arbitrary and were based on early full‑length 16S comparisons; they don’t always match the true taxonomic lineage. classify‑consensus‑vsearch (or classify‑consensus‑blast) builds a consensus from the lineages of the top hits instead of applying a hard identity cut‑off. The plugin outputs a search‑results table that can be tabulated with qiime metadata tabulate if you want to apply your own thresholding. Raising --p-perc-identity will only keep hits that meet that identity, so setting it too high can drop ASVs that lack a close match. Instead, you can change maxhits or use the top-hits-only flag to control how many top hits contribute to the consensus. Using a low --p-perc-identity such as 0.7 means that if the best hit is only 70 % identical, it is unlikely to be correct, but you will normally get several hits above that value and the consensus lineage will be derived from them. You can always inspect the search‑results output to verify any questionable assignments.

instruction: Explain whether Greengenes2 is recommended for taxonomy classification compared to SILVA and whether SILVA remains a suitable database.
input: SILVA, Greengenes2

output: It is not possible to feed more than one primer pair into qiime feature-classifier extract-reads. If you want an amplicon-specific classifier, make two separate classifiers, one for V3V4 and another for V4. Also consider reading related posts about caveats of combining variable regions. Extract separate V4 and V3V4 amplicon regions from full-length marker gene data, or via RESCRIPt. PCR primer bias could be an issue when extracting V4 from V3V4 reads.

instruction: Check if the taxonomic classification from SILVA 138 using QIIME2 is normal or if the group names are incorrectly assigned as phylum, and determine how to correct it.
input: silva-138-99-nb-classifier.qza; get-silva-data --p-version '138.1' --p-target 'SSURef_NR99'; reverse-transcribe; cull-seqs; filter-seqs-length-by-taxon --p-labels Eukaryota --p-min-lens 1400; dereplicate --p-mode 'uniq'; fit-classifier-naive-bayes; classify-sklearn --p-confidence 0.9; metadata tabulate
output: The behavior is expected. When SILVA taxonomic information is missing at a rank, get‑silva‑data propagates the information from the previous rank. In these cases, the class or order information is missing, so the phylum label (Labyrinthulomycetes) is repeated at those ranks. Labyrinthulomycetes is actually a class but is listed in SILVA as a phylum for these accessions. You can disable this rank propagation by using the --p-no-propagate-option with get‑silva‑data, which keeps the empty ranks. Alternatively, you can adjust the ranks used with the --p-ranks parameter.

instruction: Assist me in training the Silva 138 database for QIIME1, specifically obtaining consensus_taxonomy_7_levels.txt and silva_138_99_16S_515-806.fna files.
input: QIIME1, Silva 138
output: As of 1 January 2018, QIIME 2 has succeeded QIIME 1. QIIME 1 is no longer officially supported and all development and support efforts are now focused entirely on QIIME 2. That said, it should still be possible to do your taxonomic classification with QIIME 2. You can import your QIIME 1 representative sequences file to QIIME 2 (see "importing" tutorials at the website below), classify with one of the pre-trained SILVA classifiers (see the website below), and then export the data for further analysis (even in QIIME 1 if you wish). You can refer to https://qiime2.org to learn how to install and start using QIIME 2.

instruction: Create a pipeline for 16S sequences that reconciles sequence assignments across multiple databases, exposing ambiguities in a CSV file, using NCBI as the master database.
input: NCBI, Greengenes 2
output: RESCRIPt was built to reconcile taxonomic annotations across databases; it can produce outputs that show differences (see Figure 5). There is no dedicated tool in RESCRIPt that directly maps one database to another, but you can combine RESCRIPt, QIIME 2 and other scripts to build such a pipeline. QIIME 2’s `feature-table tabulate-seqs` accepts multiple taxonomy artifacts, which lets you compare how different classifiers assign taxonomy to the same sequences. You can append multiple taxonomies to a table, though mismatching sequence IDs or differing sequence sets between databases need to be handled. GTDB also provides a search interface to match genome names. Overall, you can build a pipeline by chaining RESCRIPt, QIIME 2 and custom code, but a ready‑made tool does not exist.

instruction: Explain whether QIIME normally fills all taxonomic ranks with the same name when a taxon has uncertain lower‑rank placement, and describe how to report such taxa (for example, whether to use "Candidatus Peribacteria" as the genus).
input: 16S V3-V4, SILVA 138.1, RESCRIPt, QIIME
output: QIIME’s default behavior (when using the QIIME‑2 formatted SILVA database) is to propagate a taxonomic name to all lower ranks when the SILVA taxonomy does not provide a lower‑rank assignment. This is the default setting under the Rank‑Propagation menu in the SILVA tutorial. It can be turned off or customized if you build the database yourself. The Incertae Sedis label will appear in the rank that the SILVA curators have assigned it. You can use RESCRIPt’s edit‑taxonomy function to change the taxonomy as you wish. The term "Candidatus" indicates a provisional taxon that is somewhat supported but lacks detailed biological information, whereas "Incertae Sedis" indicates uncertain phylogenetic placement; deciding whether to report a taxon as genus "Candidatus Peribacteria" is a taxonomic judgment that is best left to taxonomists. SILVA 138.2 is available and can be installed via RESCRIPt, providing an updated taxonomy that may alter these assignments.

instruction: Provide guidance on how to build an OTU cluster using the SILVA 138.1 database and incorporate representative sequences for phylogenetic tree construction in QIIME2, given that SILVA only provides FASTA format and cannot generate a table.qza via demultiplexing.
input: QIIME2, SILVA 138.1, RESCRIPt, qiime vsearch cluster-features-de-novo --p-perc-identity 0.99, FASTA format
output: SILVA sequences are only available in FASTA format, not FASTQ. You should generate OTUs/ASVs from your data (which will be in FASTA) and then append them to representative sequences from SILVA. A quicker approach is to use QIIME2’s fragment insertion to insert your 18S sequences into an existing phylogeny using the SEEP files. If you want to create a new reference tree, try the notebook provided. Use the latest SILVA v138.2 and install the newest RESCRIPt from GitHub with: conda activate qiime2-amplicon-2024.5; pip install git+https://github.com/bokulich-lab/RESCRIPt.git; qiime dev refresh-cache.

instruction: Determine whether the taxonomy assignment is correct and explain why E. coli may not appear in the taxonomy file.
input: QIIME 2, qiime tools import, qiime cutadapt trim-paired, qiime dada2 denoise-paired, qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn
output: E. coli is a species, and even a full‑length 16S rRNA sequence is often not enough for species‑level annotations. The 16S rRNA sequence of E. coli is very similar to sequences from other species, so the classifier often assigns it to "g__Escherichia-Shigella" instead of a species label.

instruction: Identify whether the 2024.5 SILVA classifier file is valid and explain how to ensure consistent classification results across different SILVA classifiers when using QIIME2 feature-classifier on PacBio HiFi data, including the use of the same reference database, provenance checking, and understanding weighted classifiers.
input: feature-classifier, VSEARCH, DADA2, silva-138-99-nb-classifier_202405.qza, shasum -a 256, PacBio machine, HiFi_16S nf workflow, scikit-learn version error
output: Mixing different versions of SILVA and other databases (e.g., GTDB v207, SILVA v138, SILVA v138.1, SILVA v138.2) leads to inconsistent results. Use the same reference database for all comparisons. SILVA has been updated to 138.2; you can use RESCRIPt in QIIME 2 2024.10 to get premade files or curate your own. The weighted classifiers are generated differently (often pulling from QIITA) and will give different taxonomic distributions. Check the provenance tab in QIIME 2 View to see which SILVA version each file was built from. Ensuring consistent database version and understanding weighted vs. standard classifiers will resolve the discrepancies.

instruction: Determine which Greengenes folder contains the reference sequences for training a classifier, explain why the other folders are present, and describe why training a classifier is necessary and common across taxonomic analysis tools.
output: Use the rep_set folder for reference sequences, such as rep_set/99_otus.fasta together with taxonomy/99_otu_taxonomy.txt, to train the classifier. The rep_set_aligned folder holds aligned sequences, which are not required by current QIIME 2 feature classifiers but might be needed by future ones. The otus folder contains OTU maps that link sequences to Greengenes OTUs, and the trees folder holds phylogenetic trees derived from the aligned representatives, useful for phylogenetic diversity metrics like UniFrac when using closed‑reference OTU picking. Training a classifier is a general machine‑learning practice; for example, the RDP naive Bayes classifier must be trained on reference data. In QIIME 2, the classify‑sklearn command implements a similar trained naive‑Bayes classifier, while consensus‑based classifiers (classify‑consensus‑blast, classify‑consensus‑vsearch) do not require training. Whether a training step is needed depends on the chosen classification algorithm.

instruction: Explain how to get ITOL to recognize internal IDs and annotations in a 97_otus tree and how to display genus‑level bar charts using Greengenes trees.
input: 97_otus tree, ITOL, Greengenes
output:
Export the QIIME 2 artifacts for the tree and taxonomy:


qiime tools export rooted-tree.qza --output-dir .
qiime tools export taxonomy.qza --output-dir .


Create an annotation file (taxonomy.txt) with the required header and the taxonomic data, e.g.:


echo $'LABELS
SEPARATOR TAB
DATA' > taxonomy.txt
sed "1d" taxonomy.tsv | cut -f1,2 >> taxonomy.txt


Load the NWK file in ITOL and drag‑and‑drop the taxonomy.txt file onto the rendered tree.

For genus‑level bar charts, use the taxa‑bar‑plots visualization in QIIME 2, selecting the genus level from the drop‑down (level 6 in Greengenes). In ITOL you can display bar charts at the tips of the tree. ITOL also supports uploading QZA files directly or a ZIP file containing *‑tree.qza, taxonomy.qz a, and optionally taxa‑bar‑plots.qzv, which it will parse to label the tree and generate confidence bar charts.

instruction: Determine if the no space left error is caused by the large size of the merged reference sequence and taxonomy files, and decide whether to train separate classifiers for each database.
input: qiime, feature-classifier, ref-seqs.qza, ref-taxonomy.qza, [Errno 28] No space left on device
output: Do not merge databases that target different marker genes; keep Greengenes, SILVA, and UNITE separate and use them with the appropriate marker gene amplicons. Merging them creates noise and increases size, which can cause the No space left error. Greengenes and UNITE are usually small enough to train on a laptop, whereas SILVA alone is often too large; pre-trained classifiers are available to avoid training. Training each database separately will reduce redundancy, lower computational demands, and improve classification quality.

instruction: How can I get the 97% GG tree that has branch lengths? Also is there a version I can download that is already a qza file?
output:
Use the resources page to download the newest version of Greengenes, which includes trees with branch lengths. If you need a QZA file you can import the tree with QIIME2: `qiime tools import --input-path 97_otus.tree --output-path unrooted-tree.qza --type 'Phylogeny[Rooted]'` (or use `Phylogeny[Unrooted]` if you prefer). If the tree contains nodes with missing branch lengths you will see the error “All non-root nodes in tree must have a branch length”. To resolve this, load the tree with skbio, set a length of 0.0 for any node where length is None, then export as a QZA: 


import skbio

t = skbio.TreeNode.read('97_otus.tree')
for n in t.traverse():
    if n.length is None:
        n.length = 0.0

import qiime2
ar = qiime2.Artifact.import_data('Phylogeny[Rooted]', t)
ar.save('97_otus.qza')

instruction: Determine if using the Greengenes 13_8 99% OTUs full-length sequences without training a classifier is accurate, locate the 99_otus.fasta and 99_otu_taxonomy.txt files, and explain p-trunc-len for primer 27f/534f 16S gene.
input: Greengenes 13_8 99% OTUs, q2-feature-classifier, p-trunc-len, primer 27f/534f
output: You should train a classifier based on your specific primers instead of using the raw Greengenes OTUs. Review the training classifier tutorial to learn how to do this. The Greengenes 13_8 download includes the 99_otus.fasta and 99_otu_taxonomy.txt files, which are the files you need, not the 85_ versions. Pre‑trained classifiers are also available if your primers match those listed on the site. For p-trunc-len, refer to the tutorial explanation and also read the note box regarding paired‑end versus single‑end reads.

instruction: Help troubleshoot why core-metrics-phylogenetic fails when using the Greengenes 13_8 tree and how to match feature IDs with tree tip labels.
input: core-metrics-phylogenetic, gemelli plugin, feature classifier, feature-table group, phylogeny filter-table, feature-table rename-ids, taxa collapse, Greengenes 13_8
output: The IDs in your feature table and the labels in the tree do not match. Because of this mismatch, the phylogeny filter-table command removes all features, and core-metrics-phylogenetic reports an error. The solution is to use a tree whose tip labels correspond to the feature IDs in your table. You can either build your own tree in QIIME2 so that the IDs match, or rename the tree tip labels to match your feature IDs. Tutorials are available for building a tree in QIIME2 and for inserting your sequences into a pre‑computed tree, which will allow you to use the Greengenes tree correctly.

instruction: Use the Picrust2 plugin to train a classifier for the V3-V4 region with 99% identity using the latest Greengenes 2022 release, identify the appropriate files from the Greengenes 2022.10 release, and use QIIME2 outputs as input for PICRUSt2.
input: Picrust2, QIIME2, Greengenes-2022
output:
Use the QIIME2 feature-classifier plugin to extract reads from the Greengenes backbone sequences for the V3‑V4 region, then train a Naive Bayes classifier on the extracted reads and the associated taxonomy. The relevant Greengenes 2022.10 files are:

- 2022.10.backbone.full-length.fna.qza
- 2022.10.backbone.tax.qza

Run the following command to extract the primer‑specific reads (replace the primer sequences with your actual primers if needed):


qiime feature-classifier extract-reads \
  --i-sequences 2022.10.backbone.full-length.fna.qza \
  --p-f-primer GTGGTGGTGGTGGTGGTG \
  --p-r-primer GGACTGGACTGGACTGGA \
  --p-min-length 100 \
  --p-max-length 600 \
  --o-reads gg_12_10_ref_primer_region_seqs.qza


Then train the classifier with:


qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes \
  --i-reference-reads gg_12_10_ref_primer_region_seqs.qza \
  --i-reference-taxonomy 2022.10.backbone.tax.qza \
  --o-classifier gg_12_10_primer_region-classifier.qza


These steps create a classifier that can be used with Picrust2. The 99% identity is inherent in the Greengenes 2022.10 database; the commands above do not alter identity. The confidence column in taxonomy files reflects annotation confidence, not database sequence identity.

instruction: Decide whether to train the Greengenes classifier on the full V3‑V4 amplicon or on truncated forward reads, find a method to extract reads up to 2‑300 bp from the forward primer, and explain why many reads remain unassigned or are only classified to kingdom level when using a classifier trained on the whole amplicon with truncated reads.
input: DADA2, Greengenes
output: Use the QIIME 2 feature‑classifier extract‑reads command with the --p-trunc-len option to pull reads of a specified length from the forward primer. If many reads are unassigned or only reach the kingdom level, it may be because the reads are in mixed orientation or still contain primer sequences. A workaround is to run qiime feature‑classifier classify‑consensus‑vsearch, which ignores read orientation, or to trim primers with cutadapt before classification.

instruction: Where is the best discussion forum for the GreenGenes 2 bioinformatics database? How can I find semantics for these genera, including isolation source, prior assignment, effects in humans or animals, and distribution for human biomes?
input: GreenGenes 2
output: Hi @pone, the primary discussion forum for the GreenGenes 2 database is the QIIME2 forum. The Greengenes2 taxonomy is based on GTDB and the Living Tree Project, and it does not introduce new names; it only uses unique suffixes to differentiate polyphyletic labels. For more information on specific genera such as SFMI01, visit the GTDB website. Many of these taxa are candidate taxa, so their semantics (e.g., pathogenicity, ecological role) are often not well characterized. To investigate further, you can query Redbiom or examine datasets from AGP/Microsetta to see where the taxa appear and whether their presence correlates with phenotypes. If you are working with 16S V4 data, take the ASV, query Redbiom, and look for additional records. For shotgun data, you can pull samples based on the OGU and refine the analysis accordingly. If you want to know whether a Greengenes2 genus maps to an older genus name, you can check GTDB for remapping references.

instruction: Identify the genomics databases and bioinformatics pipelines that provide the best accuracy at the genus level for Illumina FASTQ data from a human stool sample, and indicate any research that ranks databases/pipelines by sensitivity and specificity for genus identification.
output:
Two papers are cited as starting points: 
- "Optimizing taxonomic classification of marker‑gene amplicon sequences with QIIME 2’s q2‑feature‑classifier plugin" (BioRxiv). 
- "Beating Naive Bayes at Taxonomic Classification of 16S rRNA Gene Sequences" (Frontiers).
Both papers discuss the problem of over‑classification and the limits of short‑read marker‑gene data.
They recommend using QIIME 2 and the q2‑feature‑classifier for classification.
For databases, Greengenes 2 is mentioned, but its limited coverage is noted.
Silva 138.1 is suggested as a widely used, well‑established reference database, especially for human gut samples.
The answers highlight that no single database or pipeline is perfect; accuracy depends on the biology of the 16S region and the taxonomy model.
Research comparing sensitivity and specificity is implied by the cited papers, but no explicit ranking tables are provided.
Overall, the best practice is to use QIIME 2 with a stable database such as Silva 138.1, while being aware of over‑classification issues and the lack of a true ground truth in microbial taxonomy.

instruction: Help me find the latest version of the classifier gg-13-8-99-515-806-nb-classifier.qza because I see 'UNCLASSIFIED CLOSTRIDIACEAF' at genus level.
input: gg-13-8-99-515-806-nb-classifier.qza
output: You're correct. A new page in the QIIME 2 Library now hosts updated versions of Greengenes2 along with SILVA, GTDB, and other databases, all trained for the nb‑classifier. Greengenes 2013_8 (gg_13_8) is no longer provided, as it has been deprecated. The page lists different pre‑trained classifiers, each noting the scikit‑learn version used for training, so you can select the one compatible with your environment. Note that using a classifier built with a newer scikit‑learn (e.g., 1.4.2) may require more memory; 64 GB of RAM is recommended to avoid KILLED errors. If you need to train the classifier yourself, it can be computationally intensive, and with only 16 GB of RAM it may fail.

instruction: Use q2-greengenes2 taxonomy-from-features or taxonomy-from-table commands with V4 sequences obtained after cutadapt/dada2 steps, but encounter a plugin error 'No requested tips found'. Provide advice to solve this issue.
input: q2-greengenes2, V4, cutadapt, dada2, q2 feature-classifier classify-sklearn, pretrained 2022.10.backbone.v4.nb.qza, greengenes2 taxonomy-from-features, greengenes2 taxonomy-from-table, error No requested tips found, traceback
output: The error occurs because the representative sequences are all 272 nt long, while the Greengenes2 fragment placement expects 90, 100 or 150 nt fragments relative to EMP 16S 515F. Consequently, there is no exact match for the 272 nt fragments, leading to the "No requested tips found" error. The recommended workaround is to use the classify‑sklearn output (which you already produced) as a FeatureData[Taxonomy] artifact. Alternatively, trim the representative sequences during denoising to 90, 100 or 150 nt so that they match the Greengenes2 phylogeny and then run taxonomy-from-features.

instruction: Use the greengenes2 plugin in QIIME2-2023.2 to filter and classify ASVs for V4 microbiome data and troubleshoot the 'No requested tips found' error.
input: qiime greengenes2 filter-features, qiime greengenes2 taxonomy-from-table, qiime greengenes2 taxonomy-from-features, qiime dada2 denoise-paired, wget
output: Ask for denoising parameters and primers. The ASVs may not be directly represented in Greengenes2; typical ASVs are 90, 100, and 150 nt from 515F, and only the forward read is placed. Check if the primers are 505F or 515F. If using 515F, avoid trimming the 5' end and truncate the 3' end so the ASVs match the lengths commonly placed. ASVs can be classified with classify-sklearn using the full-length model for the 515F-806R region, or they can be clustered against Greengenes2 with the non-v4-16s action, which runs q2-vsearch's cluster-features-closed-reference against the Greengenes2 backbone. This approach succeeded for the user.

instruction: Explain how to resolve mismatched taxonomy table format causing phyloseq object error when reanalyzing data with Greengenes2 in Qiime2 and exporting to R.
input: Error in validObject(.Object) : invalid class “phyloseq” object: Component taxa/OTU names do not match. Qiime2, Greengenes2, phyloseq, R
output: If you use both the Greengenes2 package and Qiime2 together, the outputs will have different ID formats and cause disparities. Use only the Qiime2 classifier to generate taxonomy. Closed reference clustering (q2-vsearch) reduces the number of features and does not expose mapping information, so a lower count of taxonomy rows is normal. Follow the Qiime2 classifier tutorial and use pre‑computed Naive Bayes classifiers for Greengenes2 (available in the data resources). After classification, run the taxonomy‑from‑table command to align taxonomy with your ASV table, then export the table to R. This will give you matching taxa/OTU names and avoid the phyloseq object error.

instruction: Explain why Faith PD values derived from the GG2 tree are much higher than previously calculated values and whether this is expected.
input: GG2 plugin, fragment-insertion, GG2 tree(2022.10.phylogeny.asv.nwk.qza), human stool 150nt V4 16S samples
output: The Faith PD values are higher because the branch lengths in the GG2 tree are scaled to the phylogenomic backbone and normalized to express PD as a proportion of the total tree, reflecting the massive complexity of the GG2 backbone.

instruction: Explain why the phylogenetic method loses many reads compared to the Naïve Bayes classifier and recommend which method to use.
input: Greengenes2, Naïve Bayes classification, phylogenetic classification, V1-V2 region, 16s rRNA, paired-end reads, 320bp reads after denoising
output: The phylogenetic method only places V4 ASVs (90–150 nt fragments). Your V1‑V2 data will not map, so most reads are discarded. This is a limitation of the method, not a database issue. For V1‑V2 data, use the Naïve Bayes full‑length classifier or closed‑reference clustering with the non‑v4‑16s action.

instruction: Run the R command read_qza to import a QZA tree file and resolve the memory allocation error.
input: qiime2R, read_qza, could not allocate memory (2048 Mb), R_AllocStringBuf, slurm --mem=64G
output: Memory allocation errors usually mean the system does not have enough RAM. Check the amount of memory your computer or VM has and consider using a machine or an HPC queue with more memory. When submitting with Slurm, ensure the allocation request is correct, e.g. --mem=64G. If the tree is very large (over 20 million vertices) the 64 GB may still be insufficient; in that case you can subset the phylogeny to the feature table before loading it into R or use the QIIME command ‘phylogeny filter-tree’ to reduce the tree size.

instruction: Decide whether to use the Greengenes2 backbone data or the full data for Naive Bayes taxonomic classification of V3V4 data, and determine whether the --p-rank-handles option can be omitted for Silve and GG2 when using Rescript for dereplication after V3V4 truncation.
input: Rescript, --p-rank-handles, Greengenes2, Silva
output: The --p-rank-handles parameter still exists but has been generalized. It now accepts any set of commonly used taxonomic ranks, with defaults set to domain, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species. If you want to use the standard 6‑7 ranks that are typical for SILVA, you can keep the defaults. If you need more or fewer ranks, list the ranks you want to use. Be sure the ranks you specify are present in the reference database. The old Greengenes used Kingdom instead of Domain; Greengenes2 likely uses Domain. Thus you don't need to omit the option; just adjust the ranks as needed.

instruction: Use QIIME2 2023.9 Amplicon to train and apply a Greengenes2 classifier on MiSeq V4 data, extract reads, fit the classifier, classify reads, troubleshoot issues with primer retention, filter-features, and achieve taxonomic classification down to Level 7.
input: qiime, feature-classifier extract-reads, fit-classifier-naive-bayes, classify-sklearn, 2022.10.backbone.full-length.fna.qza, 2022.10.taxonomy.asv.tsv.qza, repseqs.qza, GTGYCAGCMGCCGCGGTAA, GGACTACNVGGGTWTCTAAT, SILVA 138
output: For V4 sequences the majority of ASVs have already been placed, so taxonomy assignment is not needed. Instead filter the feature table against Greengenes2 with filter-features and pull taxonomy from the matched ASVs. When training a new classifier, remove the primer sequences and trim only the forward read to 150 or 100 nt; this is the preferred practice and gives the best classification. Use the md5 variant of the Greengenes2 files and avoid retaining primer sequences. If greengenes2 filter-features returns “No requested tips found”, it means the reference taxonomy file does not contain the requested IDs – use the correct reference tree and table. After removing primers and using forward reads, the Naive Bayes classifier can achieve Level 7 classification. For barplot visualisation, use the feature table produced by greengenes2 filter-features (not the original DADA2 table) as the input table; this resolves the “Feature IDs found in the table are missing from the taxonomy” error. SILVA does not provide species level taxonomy. The smaller file size for Greengenes2 extract-reads (9 MB vs 16 MB for SILVA) reflects the shorter reference sequences used by Greengenes2.

instruction: Determine whether a SEPP reference database exists for Greengenes2, and if not, decide which phylogeny artifact (2022.10.phylogeny.asv.nwk.qza, 2022.10.phylogeny.id.nwk.qza, or 2022.10.phylogeny.md5.nwk.qza) should be used for phylogenetic analysis.
input: greengenes2, SEPP, phylogeny data
output: For 2022.10, Greengenes2 has placed ~20 million V4 ASVs that can be used. For non‑V4 data, fragment placement isn’t yet available; the recommended path is to use the non‑v4‑16s action to recruit the ASVs to the backbone and then use the 2022.10.phylogeny.id.nwk.qza phylogeny. It is also possible to train a SEPP model from the backbone, but that has not been tested and may be uncertain. In practice, use the 2022.10.phylogeny.id.nwk.qza file for phylogenetic analysis.

instruction: Troubleshoot the issue where the filter-features output file is empty when using QIIME2 with 16S V4 data.
input: qiime, qiime dada2 denoise-paired, qiime greengenes2 filter-features, qiime feature-classifier, qiime taxa collapse, conda, p-trunc-len-f 145, p-trunc-len-r 145
output: Check whether primer sequences are present in your reads before running DADA2; if so, remove them with cutadapt. Verify that the reference taxonomy file matches the ASV identifiers—use the 2022.10.taxonomy.asv.md5.qza file if your ASVs are md5 hashes. Also ensure that the ASV lengths (e.g., 145–150 nt) correspond to sequences represented in the Greengenes2 tree; only forward reads of the appropriate length are needed. If after these steps filter-features still produces an empty table, the issue is likely due to a mismatch between your ASV IDs and the taxonomy reference.

instruction: Find where to download the sequence and taxonomy files for GreenGenes2.
input: PacBio HiFi, PacBio's Nextflow pipeline, 16S full-length reads, GreenGenes2
output: All reference databases and classifiers can be found at the data resources page. Alternatively, you could go to the GreenGenes2 website linked in the paper. There are several different file types ready for download, and you can pick what you need. In the meantime here are the direct links you can use: Greengenes2 2022.10 full length sequences, Greengenes2 2022.10 from 515F/806R region of sequences. Apologies for any confusion caused.

instruction: Explain whether and how to merge d__Bacteria;__ and d__Bacteria;p__ taxonomic labels, and discuss whether to discard unclassified phylum-level taxa, providing guidance for QIIME2 classification with Greengenes2.
input: Greengenes2 (2022.10), qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, QIIME2 2023.9, d__Bacteria;__, d__Bacteria;p__
output: Both d__Bacteria;__ and d__Bacteria;p__ represent the same situation: a lack of phylum assignment. In practice they can be treated as the same and merged into a single ‘Unclassified’ phylum category. Many users prefer to remove such too‑general annotations. Filtering can be performed before abundance calculations, and the filtered table should be used for downstream diversity metrics as well. Example methods statements filter out ASVs without phylum‑level annotation or that cannot be placed in a phylogenetic tree. In some cases unclassified sequences are host or eukaryotic DNA; tools like Kraken2 can help remove those. Mixed orientation reads may affect classifier output; tools such as vsearch or Kraken2 are tolerant of orientation.

instruction: Explain why using the V4 classifier results in many unassigned taxa when processing V3-V4 16S rRNA data and whether using a full-length classifier is appropriate.
input: QIIME2 2024.10, GreenGene2 v2024.9, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, rep-seqs-deblur.qza, 2024.09.backbone.v4.nb.sklearn-1.4.2.qza, 2024.09.backbone.full-length.nb.sklearn-1.4.2.qza, p-n-jobs 6
output: The V4 classifier was trained only on the V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene, but you are sequencing the longer V3‑V4 region. Because the classifier does not have reference data for the full V3‑V4 sequence, many reads cannot be matched and are reported as "Unassigned." In contrast, the full‑length classifier was trained on sequences covering the entire 16S rRNA gene, so it can assign taxa to your V3‑V4 reads more accurately. Therefore, using the full‑length classifier is appropriate and will give more reliable taxonomic assignments.

instruction: Determine a method to avoid repeatedly deleting '_A' or '_number' labels when analyzing bacterial community taxonomy across ranks.
input: Greengenes
output: Those taxa are not monophyletic, and it would be misleading to remove the suffixes. GTDB uses _A, _B, etc to denote polyphyly. Within Greengenes2, some taxa are further unsupported as monophyletic even with the GTDB suffixes, and that is where the _number component comes from. The suffixes keep the taxonomy aligned with the phylogeny; if they are removed, the taxonomy will no longer reflect the phylogeny.

input: pip install q2-greengenes2, QIIME 2, greengenes2 plugin, error: QIIME 2 has no plugin/command named 'greengenes2', Ubuntu 20.04.6 LTS, 2022.10.backbone.full-length.fna.qza, 2022.10.backbone.tax.qza, feature-classifier, naïve-bayes, qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes, Greengenes2_ref_seqs.qza, Greengenes2-classifier.qza

MD5 sums are: full-length.fna.qza: eb2c35a194ca340fba4ce486c08747d3; tax.qza: 010ade1db3c86814cdd0fe61be04dc8a. Training a naïve‑bayes classifier can finish in 1‑2 hours, which is acceptable. instruction: Generate two new QIIME2 classifiers for the 150 bp dataset using the 515F 926R primers and compare the results to the existing classifier to verify that no errors were made during creation.
input: qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, fit-classifier-naive-bayes, classify-sklearn, metadata tabulate; primers GTGCCAGCMGCCGCGGTAA, CCGYCAATTYMTTTRAGTTT; parameters p-min-length 50, p-max-length 250, p-trunc-len 150
output: The issue comes from the `qiime feature-classifier extract-reads` command where the `--p-max-length` was set to 250. That value is shorter than the V4 amplicon amplified by the 515‑806 primers, so sequences longer than 250 nt are dropped. As a result the classifier is built from only a few sequences, producing mainly Streptococcus or Unassigned classifications. Increase `--p-max-length` to an appropriate value (e.g., >300 nt) and re‑run the extraction. Additionally, consider using the existing full‑length Greengenes2 classifier or training on the full‑length backbone sequences rather than fragmentary data, as this can produce unexpected effects when many fragmentary sequences are used.

instruction: Explain whether the reduction from 49,583 to 3,936 features after using greengenes2.actions.non_v4_16s is typical for closed reference OTU picking and how to determine if there is a problem with the data.
