input: mosh fondue get-all, QIIME2 artifact, Windows 11 64-bit, threads 5, retries 5
output: Depending on the size and type of data you are retrieving (i.e., amplicon vs. metagenomics), this command can take up to several days; that is a reasonable runtime range.

instruction: What programs can I use to analyse a 42 MB nanopore FASTQ file that is already barcoded?
input: nanopore sequencing, fastq file
output: QIIME 2 currently does not have official support for nanopore data. If you want to try it, first demultiplex the barcoded FASTQ using the q2‑cutadapt tutorial. Next, perform quality filtering with q2‑quality‑filter. Then dereplicate and cluster into OTUs with the q2‑vsearch OTU picking tutorial, and optionally run chimera filtering. DADA2 is not suitable for nanopore, and it is unclear whether Deblur works with this data. See the Getting Started guide and Moving Pictures tutorial for more help.

instruction: Help with running DADA2 and Deblur on nanopore 16S rRNA reads (1650 bp) in QIIME2 2019.4 on Ubuntu 16.04, encountering errors.
input: dada2, deblur, qiime2 2019.4, ubuntu 16.04, conda, R 3.5.1
output: Nanopore data is not supported by DADA2 or Deblur. You may need to process the data outside of QIIME2 or use older methods such as OTU picking in QIIME2.

instruction: Help me troubleshoot the long runtime and memory issues when running qiime phylogeny align-to-tree-mafft-fasttree on Nanopore 16S V1–V9 reads, possibly by subsampling or using flags.
input: qiime2, mafft, FastTreeMP, seqtk, subsample 5000, memory errors, Nanopore 16S V1–V9
output: Try reducing the subsample to 500 reads. Use the --p-parttree flag when running mafft if more than one million sequences. Check read error rates and ambiguous bases, as they can lead to poor alignments. Consider filtering chimeras and singletons and clustering reads at 70% identity to retain the most abundant cluster before sampling. Filter reads by length (e.g., 1250–1750 bp). Use polishing tools such as nanopolish, nanofilt, or fastp if available. If memory errors persist, use additional HPC resources or consider tools outside QIIME2 such as kraken2, minimap2, or MMseqs2.

instruction: Use Nanopore sequencing data and process it in Qiime2.
input: NEBNext Ultra II End Repair/dATailing Module, NEBNext Quick Ligation Module, NEBNext Blunt/TA Ligase Master Mix, NEBNext FFPE DNA Repair Mix, MinION Mk1B Starter Pack Native Barcoding Kit 24 V14, R10.4.1 Flow Cell, Guppy, Bonito, Porechop
output: MetONTIIME is a nanopore pipeline for Qiime2 that might be suitable for your data set. Qiime is a great fit for V3-V4 reads, stool microbiomes, and alpha & beta diversity. Let us know what you try next.

instruction: Troubleshoot the 'Missing fromPath parameter' error when running the MetONTIIME2 Nextflow pipeline on nanopore data, using the provided metontiime2.conf and metontiime2_nf files.
input: QIIME2, Illumina, MetONTIIME, nanopore, nextflow, metontiime2.nf, metontiime2_conf.txt
output: The QIIME 2 developers don’t have direct experience with MetONTIIME, but the issue appears to be in the pipeline configuration. Because the data are in /fs03, the container needs access to that directory. If you’re using the singularity profile, change line 74 from containerOptions = '--bind /home/:/home' to containerOptions = '--bind /home/:/home --bind /fs03:/fs03'. If you’re using the docker profile, change line 171 from containerOptions = '-v /home/:/home' to containerOptions = '-v /home/:/home -v /fs03:/fs03'. Next, if you already have .qza files for sequences and taxonomy, place them in /fs03/pv54/Beks/Raw‑Reads/nanopore_silvan/run1_metontiime/importDb with names matching the dbSequencesQza and dbTaxonomyQza parameters, and set importDb = false (line 53). If you prefer to start from fasta and tsv files, leave importDb = true and specify the full paths for dbSequencesFasta and dbTaxonomyTsv. Test the pipeline with the demo dataset first. If the problem persists, open an issue in the GitHub repository.

instruction: Explain how to import nanopore 16S reads into QIIME2, whether additional processing such as error correction or QC adjustments is needed, and provide a starting tutorial link.
input: QIIME 2, epi2me, Medaka, MetONTIIME
output:
To import the reads into QIIME2, use the import command:

qiime tools import \
  --type 'SampleData[SequencesWithQuality]' \
  --input-path manifest.tsv \
  --input-format 'SingleEndFastqManifestPhred33V2' \
  --output-path $DATA/nanopore.qza

The manifest.tsv file must contain two columns: 'sample-id' and 'absolute-filepath'.

After importing, you should use VSEARCH for denoising, as DADA2 is not appropriate for nanopore data. 

For guidance on how to proceed, refer to the QIIME2 user documentation and the “Moving Pictures” tutorial.

instruction: Determine how to map ASV reference sequences produced by QIIME2 into 16S full-length nanopore sequences, including deciding whether to use the ASVs as reference or query and selecting appropriate mapping parameters.
input: QIIME2, Illumina MiSeq, nanopore sequencing, minimap2
output: Import the nanopore reads into QIIME2 and dereplicate them with VSEARCH to generate a feature abundance table and a rep-seqs file. Due to the high error rate of nanopore reads, many features will be unique. For mapping, use the nanopore reads as the reference and the Illumina-derived ASVs as the query. Run minimap2 with the nanopore mode (e.g., "-x map-ont"), and use the rep-seqs.qza file converted to FASTA as the reference. The resulting mappings can then be used to create an abundance table of ASVs for the nanopore data.

instruction: Explain how to import multiple sequences of different lengths into QIIME2 or suggest alternative pipelines/programs for analyzing nanopore sequencing data.
input: QIIME2,VSEARCH
output: QIIME2 supports many sequencing types; the DADA2 plugin works with Illumina, 454, IonTorrent, and PacBio CCS data. However, variable‑length amplicons are not well supported, even on Illumina. A workflow that pools PCR products from multiple primer sets (similar to Ion Torrent multi‑region kits) is known to be difficult in QIIME2. For nanopore data, consider using the EPI2ME workflow wf‑metagenomics rather than wf‑amplicon.

instruction: How can I import nanopore reads into QIIME 2, denoise them with DADA2, and produce an ASV table?
input: qiime,dada2
output: DADA2 may not work well with nanopore reads because there are too few clean reads for the model to function, as suggested by the poor denoising statistics. It is often better to use vsearch clustering or another tool for nanopore data and then bring the results back into QIIME 2 for downstream processing.

instruction: Explain why the sequence alignment step in the provided script takes a long time and uses high CPU/RAM, and whether it is normal or something is wrong.
input: qiime vsearch cluster-features-de-novo, qiime alignment mafft, qiime phylogeny fasttree
output: If your CPU and RAM usage are both that high, then it is very likely that this step is just taxing your computer. How long have you let it run for? You may be better off attempting to run this command on a cluster if you have access to one.

instruction: Explain how to process 16S v1-v9 full region ONT data in QIIME2, including whether DADA2 can be used and step‑by‑step methods if not.
input: DADA2, QIIME2, ONT
output: DADA2 is not appropriate for nanopore data. In QIIME2 you can import the reads and dereplicate them with the vsearch plugin, which provides representative sequences and a feature table. Avoid clustering reads to 97% OTUs; long reads are not best used at 85% OTUs and clustering adds little value while taking time. After dereplication you can use the data directly for taxonomy annotation and collapse it to taxonomy levels (species or genera) for non‑phylogenetic metrics and differential abundance tests. Outside of QIIME2 other options include Kraken2 (with standard or custom databases), 16S WF Epi2Me, Natrix2 (loses many reads), and NanoCLUST (outdated). A custom pipeline called NaMeco is available. The QIIME2 metagenomic distribution includes the mosphit plugin, which can be used for Kraken2 annotation of nanopore reads with various databases; you need to extract Kraken2 reports from the QIIME artifact to obtain taxonomy abundances. For classification you can use the q2-mosphit plugin with Kraken2; a draft tutorial is available. Do not use bracken to normalize counts with long‑read nanopore data.

instruction: Troubleshoot and resolve errors in using q2-moshpit to classify shotgun sequencing data with Kraken2 and Bracken, including issues with empty Kraken2 reports, adapter removal, sequencing depth, and database selection.
input: q2-moshpit, Kraken2, Bracken, pluspf database, error: no reads found, error: no space left on device, threads, confidence, minimum-base-quality
output: First check the Kraken2 report; if it contains no species‑level hits then Bracken will fail with "no reads found". In that case adjust the level with the --p-level option (e.g., G for genus). Remove adapters before classification. The depth of data required depends on the study; consult literature. The full PlusPF database is larger but more complete; a 16 GB capped version trades some sensitivity for a smaller index. Finally, ensure sufficient disk space, as the pipeline can run out of temp space.

instruction: Create a filtering functionality for kraken2 report files to avoid errors when generating feature tables with qiime moshpit estimate-bracken, and assess whether it makes sense and is easy to implement.
input: kraken2, qiime moshpit estimate-bracken, filter samples
output: We are currently discussing adding a filter action for Kraken reports to remove samples with no classified reads. An issue has been opened on q2-moshpit (github.com/bokulich-lab/q2-moshpit) to add this feature. It should be possible, but we do not yet have an ETA.

instruction: Add the metaphlan2 plugin to q2
input: q2, q2-metaphlan2, metaphlan2, metaphlan3, qiime2
output: q2-metaphlan2 is outdated and not compatible with new QIIME 2 versions. Developers will not support metaphlan2, but may add metaphlan3 in future. You could try installing an earlier version of QIIME 2 to get q2-metaphlan2 working, or use metaphlan3 as a standalone outside of QIIME 2.

instruction: Install the metaphlan2 plugin into the Qiime2 environment for q2Galaxy using Podman.
input: metaphlan2 plugin, Qiime2 environment, q2Galaxy, Podman
output: Unfortunately we don't have a good official way of adding new plugins to q2galaxy. It should be possible if you set up your own Galaxy deployment manually and then use q2galaxy to generate the tools for the plugin(s) you want and host q2galaxy locally, but it may be pretty obnoxious and involved.

instruction: Enable multithreading for the qiime moshpit eggnog-annotate function by using an appropriate parameter.
input: qiime2-shotgun-2024.2, q2-moshpit, eggnog-diamond-search, eggnog-annotate
output: Use the --p-num-cpus parameter for multiprocessing in eggnog-annotate, which was added in the 2024.5 version of the Q2 metagenome distribution.

instruction: Create a FeatureData[MAG] artifact from a SampleData[MAGs] artifact for use with dereplicate and classify-kraken2 commands in QIIME 2 moshpit.
input: qiime moshpit bin-contigs-metabat, --o-mags, SampleData[MAGs], qiime moshpit classify-kraken2, FeatureData[MAG], sourmash
output: SampleData[MAGs] represents non‑dereplicated MAGs, while FeatureData[MAG] corresponds to dereplicated MAGs. To produce FeatureData[MAG], first generate a DistanceMatrix of similarity between all MAGs in the SampleData artifact—this can be done using the QIIME 2 sourmash plugin. Then feed that DistanceMatrix into the dereplicate action of q2-moshpit to obtain a FeatureData[MAG] artifact. Documentation is limited, but a tutorial is available.

instruction: Explain whether Bracken can accept SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('contigs')] as input, and describe the expected behavior regarding contig versus read input for Bracken.
input: qiime assembly assemble-spades, qiime moshpit classify-kraken2, qiime moshpit estimate-bracken, qiime-metagenome-2024.10, spades_contigs.qza, kraken_db.qza, kraken_report.qza, bracken_db.qza, SampleData[Contigs], SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('contigs')], SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('reads')], error: Invalid value for '--i-kraken-reports': Expected an artifact of at least type SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('reads')]. An artifact of type SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('contigs')] was provided.
output: Bracken requires read information and cannot use contigs. Therefore you can only feed the output from classification of your reads, not the contigs. Bracken uses read length to estimate abundances, so it expects SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('reads')] and not SampleData[Kraken2Report % Properties('contigs')].

instruction: Run the moshpit-dev tutorial commands and resolve the QIIME 2 error stating that the plugin 'moshpit' does not exist.
input: moshpit-dev, q2-moshpit, q2-annotate, QIIME 2
output: The q2-moshpit plugin has been renamed to q2-annotate. In the latest moshpit-dev conda environment, use the 'annotate' plugin instead.

instruction: Explain how to resolve the error when running mosh annotate filter-reads-pangenome in QIIME2 moshpit due to a retired NCBI Datasets API.
input: mosh annotate filter-reads-pangenome, NCBI Datasets API (410 Gone)
output: The error is caused by a service used to fetch human genomes that is no longer supported. While this is being addressed, you can use a human reference genome (GRCh37 or GRCh38) as the filter. Build a Bowtie2 index from the genome fasta file using the bowtie2_build action from q2-quality-control, and then provide that index as the --o-reference-index input to the mosh annotate filter-reads-pangenome command.

instruction: Determine whether to delete MAGs not annotated as at least "d__Bacteria" or "d__Archaea" before functional annotation or to use all dereplicated MAGs regardless of annotation.
output: For bulk functional annotation, drop any MAGs not confidently classified as "d__Bacteria" or "d__Archaea", unless you are specifically targeting novel lineages. This keeps your annotations focused on bona fide prokaryotes and conserves computational resources. After annotation, subset KO annotations to the pathways of interest, extract KOs that originate from Bacteria and Archaea, and filter the KO table for differential abundance analyses to avoid strong penalties from multiple comparisons of KOs not from Bacteria or Archaea.

instruction: Import MAGs from a fasta file into the MOSHPIT pipeline using the correct command and file organization.
input: mosh tools cache-import, --cache ./cache, --key mags, --type FeatureData[MAGs], --input-path data/mags, Semantic type FeatureData[MAGs] is invalid error
output:
Use the SampleData[MAGs] type instead of FeatureData[MAGs] and specify the MultiFASTADirectoryFormat. Organize the fasta file inside a per‑sample directory (e.g., data/mags/sample1/yourfile.fasta). Then run:

mosh tools cache-import --type SampleData[MAGs] --input-format MultiFASTADirectoryFormat --input-path data/mags --cache ./cache --key mags

Make sure there are no hidden files such as .DS_Store in the directories.

instruction: Download all run IDs from the moshpit tutorial dataset at once without encountering 'Failed to download sequences. Error: This sra toolkit installation has not been configured' errors, and resolve issues preventing viewing of qzv files.
input: "--p-threads 11", "Failed to download sequences. Error: This sra toolkit installation has not been configured", vdb-config -i, FutureWarning, fasterq-dump quit with error code 3, cannot find SRR
output: Read the next line in the output; it will tell you exactly what to do. Use the documentation link provided. Run 'vdb-config -i' to configure the SRA toolkit as instructed in the Wiki. The FutureWarning is only a warning and does not affect functionality. Slow downloads are normal for metagenome data. If downloads fail with 'fasterq-dump quit with error code 3' or 'cannot find SRR...', check firewall settings or proxy configuration. If behind a proxy, configure it as described in the q2-fondue documentation. If you have local data, use 'mosh fondue combine-seqs' instead of 'mosh fondue get-all'.

instruction: Regenerate the contigs_results_table.qza with existing contigs and merge multiple contig tables if needed, and find a way to extract information from a large contigs.qzv visualisation.
input: MOSHPIT, parsl, artifact cache, mosh assembly evaluate-quast, QIIME 2
output: To regenerate the contigs_results_table.qza you can import the existing contigs into a new SampleData[Contigs] artifact and run evaluate-quast again. Merging multiple QUASTResults artifacts is not supported, but you can merge the resulting tables with qiime metadata merge, e.g., qiime metadata merge --m-metadata1-file contigs_results_table1.qza --m-metadata2-file contigs_results_table2.qza --o-merged-metadata merged_contigs_results.qza. The merged artifact is of ImmutableMetadata type and can be used in actions that accept metadata. To get the data from the large visualisation, use qiime tools export to unpack into a directory containing TSV tables and PDFs; you can also run evaluate with --p-no-icarus to reduce the size by disabling the Icarus browser plots.

instruction: Explain the steps to convert VMH diet files to MICOM format, how taxa mapping between databases is handled, whether Greengenes and GTDB are supported, and how to run per-sample growth simulations using a manifest file.
input: micom, q2-micom, VMH, AGORA, SILVA, Greengenes, GTDB, QIIME 2
output: Convert VMH diet files to MICOM by: converting units to mmol/gDW/h, adjusting IDs for gut, applying dilution factors for components absorbed in the small intestine, and adding any missing growth‑supporting components. The micom media repository on GitHub contains notebooks that perform this conversion; a pre‑converted VMH diet is already available. For taxonomic mapping, the earlier approach matched by name but is now replaced by a Refseq 16S classifier (e.g., 515f‑805r) to map SILVA/16S to AGORA strains. GTDB support is planned but not yet complete; mapping of AGORA strains to GTDB is underway. MICOM supports per‑sample media; you can supply a per‑sample medium artifact via the plugin. Use the provided gist or plugin interface to supply a manifest that links each subject to their own .qza medium, then run `qiime micom grow` with that media. Note that media with more nutrients will yield higher growth rates and fluxes.

instruction: Explain how to build a genus-level metabolic model in micom-q2, including which species are included, whether all reactions are merged or selected, criteria for selection, and the tool to use for merging.
input: micom-q2, AGORA, q2-micom plugin
output: A genus model in MICOM is a pan‑genus model that includes the union of all metabolic reactions of the strains for that genus; the biomass composition is the average of all strains. All strains in AGORA are covered by the model database provided in the tutorial; genera that are not present in AGORA are not covered. All reactions from the species are included; there is no need to select a subset. The tool for merging is MICOM itself – you can merge models via the database builder included in the q2‑micom plugin or, if needed, ad‑hoc when using the single model API in Python (though this is not available in q2‑micom). It is recommended to build the model database first because that is faster and more reproducible. For mouse gut modeling AGORA was built from human gut strains and may be suboptimal; instead use the CARVEME database of automatic reconstructions of all Refseq genomes, which should cover the most common genera in the mouse gut better. CARVEME_genus files can be downloaded from Zenodo; a human gut diet specification adapted for the CARVEME database is available in the micom‑dev/media repository.

instruction: Use a community model for elasticities in MICOM with an Excel taxonomy file and an SBML .xml file; explain how to proceed.
input: MICOM, Excel taxonomy file, SBML .xml file
output: The taxonomy file shows how much of each taxon is present. For each taxon there is a separate model built from SBML. For the Qiime interface you need Qiime artifacts; however, you can use the Python interface to build community models by reading your table with pandas.

instruction: Troubleshoot the Qiime2 MICOM build error "cannot convert float NaN to integer" by examining abundance data and related files.
input: q2cli, qiime2, q2_micom, build, abundanceOTU4.qza, taxonomy_cleaned.qza, metabolic_models_2024.qza, ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer, plugin error from micom
output: Based on the error, it appears you have missing abundance values in your input data. I suggest replacing your NaN (missing) values with zeros in your abundance matrix, and seeing if that helps.

instruction: Suggest a similar function or workaround in QIIME2 for excluding non-target DNA.
input: exclude_seqs_by_blast.py, e-value
output: There is no built‑in QIIME2 tool that replaces QIIME1's exclude_seqs_by_blast.py. The QIIME2 developers plan to add this functionality in the 2017.8 release. As a workaround you can run the QIIME1 script on the same machine. First export your FeatureData[Sequences] artifact to a FASTA file with `qiime tools export rep‑set.qza --output-dir rep‑set`. Activate a QIIME1 conda environment and run `exclude_seqs_by_blast.py` on the exported FASTA; the script produces a file called matching.fna that contains the sequences you want to keep. Convert this FASTA to a list of feature identifiers: `grep '^>' matching.fna | cut -c 2- > seqs‑to‑keep.txt`. Then activate your QIIME2 environment and filter the feature table with `qiime feature-table filter-features --m-metadata-file seqs‑to‑keep.txt ...` to remove all features that did not match the BLAST database. In the 2017.10 release QIIME2 also includes a new plugin, q2-quality-control, which provides a command `qiime quality-control exclude-seqs` that offers similar functionality to QIIME1's script.

instruction: Filter chloroplast, mitochondria, and other unwanted sequences from a featuretable.biom generated in QIIME 1.9.1 and use it for alpha and beta diversity analysis in QIIME 2, ensuring rooted-tree.qza and table.qza are free of these sequences.
input: Qiime 2017.7, Qiime 1.9.1, qiime taxa collapse, qiime diversity core-metrics, rooted-tree.qza, table.qza, --i-phylogeny, --i-table, --p-sampling-depth 1109, featuredata[sequences], featuretable[frequency]
output: Export the feature table as a QIIME 1-compatible .biom file using qiime tools export. Filter chloroplast, mitochondria, and other unwanted sequences in QIIME 1 (e.g., with filter_otus_from_otu_table.py). Import the filtered .biom back into QIIME 2 as a FeatureTable[Frequency] artifact with qiime tools import and supply it to the core-metrics command via --i-table. For the phylogenetic tree, export the representative sequences (FeatureData[Sequence]) with qiime tools export, filter them in QIIME 1, import the filtered sequences back into QIIME 2, and build a rooted tree from them. Supply the resulting rooted tree to core-metrics via --i-phylogeny. Optionally, use QIIME 2 plugins such as q2-quality-control exclude-seqs or taxa filter-seqs for filtering representative sequences within QIIME 2.

instruction: How can I fix the 'add_field() got an unexpected keyword argument 'source'' error when running 'qiime fmt engraftment'?
input: qiime fmt engraftment
output: Check the QIIME 2 version, machine, installation method, full command and verbose error output. The error is a known bug that has been fixed in the latest QIIME 2 release. Install QIIME 2 2023.2 to avoid the issue. Note that the engraftment output does not represent a proportion of donor sample engrafted; for proportional engraftment use 'qiime fmt sample-peds'. A taxa table cannot be used for the engraftment pipeline, though it can be used with sample-peds.

instruction: Determine appropriate methods for standardizing or normalizing presence/absence microbiome data, or assess whether such processing is necessary.
output: Normalization methods for presence/absence data are not well established, because the data are binary. One can use a threshold to convert relative abundance into 1s and 0s, but that loses information. Some alpha and beta diversity metrics use binary data internally, such as Unweighted UniFrac, which ignores relative abundance and highlights rare taxa. For alpha diversity, observed OTUs can be used. For beta diversity, Jaccard distance can be used. Unweighted UniFrac may also work for microarray data, providing phylogenetic context.

instruction: Describe how to handle overrepresented sequences and DADA2 merging errors in a 16S V3-V4 paired‑end dataset with varying read lengths within QIIME2.
input: DADA2, FastQC, trunc_len_f, trunc_len_r, max_ee, trunc_q, forward read length 260, reverse read length 134
output: DADA2 does not remove overrepresented sequences because duplicate amplicon reads are expected and are used for abundance estimation. FastQC flags overrepresented sequences, but this is normal for amplicon data and does not indicate a problem. No additional pre‑processing is required before running QIIME2 beyond the usual DADA2 trimming step. If merging fails because the forward and reverse read lengths are very different, the data can be processed as single‑end or the DADA2 truncation lengths can be adjusted to accommodate the read lengths. In short, duplicate sequences are expected, FastQC overrepresented flags can be ignored for amplicons, and DADA2 trimming and parameter tuning are the primary steps needed to resolve merging failures.

instruction: Analyze all 240 Illumina MiSeq 2x250 samples for per-base sequence quality and per-base N content, focusing on a drop in quality and increased N at position 6 in reverse reads, and determine whether this is a concern, using a tool similar to FastQC.
input: Illumina MiSeq 2x250, fastqc
output: Use Qiime2’s demux summarize to obtain a single plot that summarizes quality across all 240 samples. Alternatively, use vsearch fastq-stats to compute statistics, though you will need to create the graphs yourself. The observed pattern is known; you can trim the first 6 bases from the reverse read with DADA2, which is recommended. Other tools such as MultiQC and AfterQC can also be employed for summary.

instruction: How can I start QIIME2 with fasta data that has already had primers, low quality base and barcode removed?
output: QIIME 2 cannot import FASTA files before the denoising or OTU‑picking step. It can only import FASTA files that are representative sequences of features in a feature table, so a feature table must first be generated. The current workaround is to use QIIME 1 (or another tool) to perform OTU picking, then import the resulting feature table and representative sequences into QIIME 2. Upcoming releases (2017.9) will add vsearch‑based OTU picking and upstream FASTA support.

instruction: Create a FeatureTable from Illumina FASTA sequences denoised by QIIME1, using QIIME2.
input: Illumina FASTA, QIIME1, QIIME2, OTU picking, deblur, SampleData[Sequences]
output: First import your FASTA file as SampleData[Sequences] using qiime tools import --type 'SampleData[Sequences]' --input-path seqs.fna --output-path seqs.qza. Then run qiime vsearch dereplicate-sequences on the SampleData[Sequences] artifact. This will produce a FeatureTable[Frequency] and FeatureData[Sequence]. You can then optionally cluster these dereplicated sequences into OTUs with qiime vsearch cluster-features-de-novo or cluster-features-closed-reference. Note that SampleData[Sequences] cannot be used with q2-deblur or DADA2 because they require quality scores. If you need to use deblur or DADA2, you must obtain FASTQ files.

instruction: Convert .fna or .fastq files into a QIIME2 artifact suitable for analysis in the Moving Pictures pipeline.
input: .fna files, .fastq files, FLASH, Qiime V1.7.0, split_libraries_fastq.py, UCHIME algorithm, Gold database
output:
Use QIIME2 to import the sequences.  For a single .fna file, run:


qiime tools import \
  --input-path seqs.fna \
  --output-path seqs.qza \
  --type SampleData[Sequences]


For paired‑end .fastq files, create a manifest CSV that lists the paths of the forward and reverse reads for each sample, then import:


qiime tools import \
  --type 'SampleData[PairedEndSequencesWithQuality]' \
  --input-path MANIFEST.csv \
  --output-path demux.qza \
  --source-format PairedEndFastqManifestPhred33


Do not concatenate the .fna files; instead work with the demultiplexed fastq data.  After import, you can join the pairs with vsearch:


qiime vsearch join-pairs \
  --i-demultiplexed-seqs demux.qza \
  --o-joined-sequences joined.qza


Summarize and filter the joined reads:


qiime demux summarize \
  --i-data joined.qza \
  --o-visualization joined_summary.qzv



qiime quality-filter q-score-joined \
  --i-demultiplexed-seqs joined.qza \
  --o-filtered-sequences filtered.qza \
  --o-filter-stats stats.qza


Proceed with a denoising method such as Deblur:


qiime deblur denoise-16S \
  --i-demultiplexed-seqs filtered.qza \
  --p-trim-length 250 \
  --o-representative-sequences rep-seqs.qza \
  --o-table table.qza \
  --o-stats deblur-stats.qza


The resulting feature table (table.qza) and representative sequences (rep-seqs.qza) can then be fed into the Moving Pictures workflow starting at the feature-table section.  If you prefer to use OTU picking, you can skip the denoising step and instead use vsearch for clustering.  All steps are traceable and preserve sample names for downstream diversity, taxonomic, and differential abundance analyses.

instruction: Use qiime2 vsearch dereplicate-sequences on a FASTA file to create a feature table, but avoid an IndexError: list index out of range.
input: qiime2, vsearch, CoDL-CH0001.qza, IndexError
output: The error is caused by the FASTA header format.  The sequence IDs contain ‘|’ characters, which vsearch cannot parse correctly.  Replace every ‘|’ in the header with a ‘-’ (or otherwise change the header format) before running vsearch dereplicate.  After correcting the headers the dereplication proceeds without the list index error.

instruction: Create a file containing taxonomy, OTU counts per sample, and fasta sequences using QIIME2.
input: qiime metadata tabulate, taxonomy.qza, table.qza, rep-seqs.qza
output: Use metadata tabulate with FeatureData[Sequence] and FeatureData[Taxonomy] to get the taxonomy column and the fasta sequence column. For the OTU counts, export the feature table with qiime tools export and convert it to a TSV using biom convert. The exported table will have features as rows and samples as columns. Then combine the exported taxonomy, sequence, and table files in a spreadsheet to create the desired one‑table format.

instruction: Export multiple .fasta files, each containing reads from only one sample, from a .qza file.
input: QIIME2, cutadapt, vsearch, quality-filter
output: Use the export command to extract fastq.gz files per sample. For example: mkdir extracted-reads; qiime tools export --input-path reads.qza --output-path extracted-reads/. Then convert the extracted fastq.gz files to fasta with any external tool.

instruction: How can I obtain a FASTA file from the trimmed QZA artifact (primers_removed_final.qza) in QIIME2 without running DADA2 or Deblur?
input: qiime, cutadapt
output: Your data is in fastq format, not fasta. Use `qiime tools export` to export the fastq files.

instruction: Investigate why demultiplexed paired-end sequences are only 301 nt long after Casava 1.8 import and decide if truncation is needed for DADA2 denoising.
input: QIIME 2, Casava 1.8 import, qiime dada2 denoise-paired, primers 27F/519R, demux.qza
output: A Casava import normally preserves sequence length; if your reads are 301 nt, the sequencing center likely trimmed them before delivery. Truncation is recommended before DADA2 because the algorithm will discard reads with low quality in later positions. Cut the reads where the average quality falls below a threshold such as Q30. For 27F/519R amplicons (approximately 454 bp), 301 bp reads overlap by about 74 bp, which is sufficient for merging. Using truncation lengths such as 296 for the forward read and 250 for the reverse read has been shown to improve taxonomy assignment. Note that truncating will drop any reads shorter than the specified truncation length.

instruction: Use QIIME 2 to import a BIOM 2.1 OTU table that contains ASV counts and sequence metadata, create a FeatureData[Sequence] artifact from the sequence information, and then use that artifact with a taxonomic classifier while keeping the FeatureTable[Frequency] for downstream analysis.
input: qiime tools import, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, qiime feature-table summarize, qiime clawback sequence-variants-from-samples, biom export-metadata, biom summarize-table, FeatureTable[Frequency], FeatureData[Sequence]
output: FASTA import is the norm. Extract the sequences from the BIOM observation metadata (under the name "Sequence") and create a FeatureData[Sequence] artifact. You can use tools such as biom export-metadata or the q2-clawback sequence-variants-from-samples command to separate the sequences from the table. Once you have a FeatureData[Sequence] artifact you can run the classifier. There is currently no built‑in way to combine the FeatureTable[Frequency] and the sequence data into a single QZA artifact; they must be imported separately and combined later.

instruction: Determine the specificity and sensitivity of taxa assignments in whole genome shotgun sequencing at various relative abundance thresholds (1%, 0.1%, 0.01%, 0.001%).
output: The accuracy of abundance estimates and taxonomic assignment depends on the metric; abundance may be accurate to 2 significant digits while genus assignment can be incorrect. Each pipeline step introduces signal loss and noise, so quantifying these effects throughout the pipeline is valuable. The CAMI challenge provides resources for evaluating these metrics. Qiime2 does not perform the benchmark directly, but studies have benchmarked taxonomic classification using Qiime2. The tax‑credit GitHub repository offers a framework for evaluating taxonomy classification accuracy with mock communities and simulated reads, includes pre‑computed results, and supports parameter sweeps and method optimization, enabling assessment of specificity and sensitivity at different abundance levels.

instruction: How can I extract the unique sequences that are identical to the reference sequence from a fasta file?
output: Assuming your fasta consists of unique sequences (ASVs or OTUs) output from a denoising/dereplication/otu picking method in QIIME2, you can extract sequences that are identical to reference sequences by using exclude-seqs. Just set the --p-perc-identity parameter to 1.0 to extract exact matches.

instruction: Identify and resolve the error caused by CRLF line endings in a FASTA file when running q2-exclude-seqs with vsearch in QIIME 2.
input: qiime2, vsearch, q2-exclude-seqs, FeatureData[Sequence]
output: The issue is caused by Windows-style CRLF line endings in the FASTA file, particularly in the header lines. VSEARCH interprets the CR characters as part of the sequence IDs, which corrupts the alignment report and leads to the "not enough values to unpack" error. Converting the file to Unix-style line endings (LF only) resolves the problem. You can do this with tools such as dos2unix or by running tr -d "\r" < file > newfile.fna.

instruction: Should I sequence blanks (new sterile swabs) to check for possible contamination before starting the QIIME2 pipeline?
input: QIIME2
output: It is good practice to always include and sequence negative controls. Typically, negative controls can be added starting from the DNA extraction step. However, if you are working with low‑biomass samples, it is advisable to include negative controls during sampling as well; for example, take a few sterile swabs, pretend to collect samples, and preserve them the same way as real samples. Many people add blanks starting from the DNA extraction because they cannot return to the sampling location. See Kim et al., 2017 (Optimizing methods and dodging pitfalls in microbiome research).

instruction: Explain the cause of the faith_pd error when running qiime diversity core-metrics-phylogenetic after filtering taxa and rebuilding the tree, and describe how to resolve it.
input: qiime diversity core-metrics-phylogenetic, qiime taxa filter table, qiime feature-table filter samples, faithpd, p-sampling-depth, metadata file, tree file
output: The error occurs because the observation IDs in the feature table are not a subset of the tree tips. This happens when the tree has been filtered but the feature table has not been updated to match. To fix it, ensure that the feature table contains only IDs that are present in the tree, or rebuild the tree to match the feature table.

instruction: Explain how to choose the perc-identity and perc-query-aligned thresholds when using exclude-seqs in qiime2, and recommend references to justify the choice.
input: qiime2, perc-identity, perc-query-aligned, exclude-seqs, SILVA, 0.97
output: 97% similarity is very strict for inclusion. The default threshold is 97% because it is suitable for filtering specific non-target sequences, such as host sequences. However, for a broad filter this threshold may be too stringent if you want to retain sequences that are less similar to the reference. If your goal is to keep only sequences that very closely match previously described sequences, 97% can be used. For a quantitative reference to set an appropriate threshold, consult the GTDB paper, which shows how sequence similarity associates with different bacterial taxonomic ranks.

instruction: Fix the dereplication error in QIIME2 vsearch command due to non-zero exit status -9.
input: vsearch, derep_fulllength, seqs.fna, exit status -9, QIIME2
output: The error is caused by vsearch running out of memory. Check available memory, ensure QIIME2 version, and use native installation or a virtual environment. Dereplication is not usually memory intensive; the solution is to run on a system with more memory. For example, 2 GB of RAM in a VirtualBox VM was insufficient, but increasing it to 7 GB allowed dereplication to complete. Subsequent QIIME2 steps should run fine on typical Illumina MiSeq data. Memory issues might appear during taxonomy classification or other steps that use a reference database, especially large ones like SILVA. Try the analysis again after increasing memory; if problems persist, search for parameter adjustments that mitigate memory usage.

instruction: Identify why two taxa are missing from the ASV table when using deblur on 18S mock communities amplified with V4 primers, and explain how to fix the issue.
input: deblur, dereplication
output: The two taxa were removed during dereplication because the deblur trim‑length filter was set to 380 nucleotides, which is longer than some of the reads in the mock community. Length filtering is applied before dereplication, so any reads shorter than the trim length are discarded, reducing the number of unique sequences that survive dereplication. To retain all taxa, set the trim length to the maximum read length present (e.g., 373) or disable trimming by using the option –trim‑length -1. Using a consistent trim length across samples avoids technical bias.

instruction: When running qiime rescript dereplicate, if two sequences share overlapping sequence identity and one is longer, are both retained? Using default --p-perc-identity 1, would both sequences be retained?
input: qiime rescript dereplicate, --p-perc-identity 1
output: If you check the help text via qiime rescript dereplicate --help, the sequences should remain separate unless you use the --p-derep-prefix flag. In that case the shortest will be subsumed into the longer sequence. You can activate that option by passing --p-derep-prefix (or setting it to TRUE). Using the flag reduces the number of sequences (e.g., from ~3.6 M to ~1.8 M).

instruction: Identify and handle mismatched FeatureIDs between sequence and taxonomy artifact files when dereplicating in QIIME2/RESCRIPt, and provide a method to filter sequences lacking corresponding taxonomy.
input: vsearch, RESCRIPt, QIIME2, get-ncbi-data, KeyError 'M27461.1'
output: get-ncbi-data may return sequences that do not have taxids. It prints a warning listing the missing accessions and bad taxids. The mismatch causes a KeyError during dereplicate. Workarounds: export the fasta from the sequences file, delete the problematic sequences, and re-import; or export the _tax.qza, list the FeatureIDs it contains, and use qiime feature-table filter-seqs with that list (or directly use the _tax.qza as the metadata file). get-ncbi-data now discards unmapped sequences; running with --verbose will show the warning.

instruction: How can I account for instances when BOLD and NCBI use different naming strategies when merging COI databases?
input: VSEARCH --derep_fulllength, get-ncbi-data
output:
- Recognize that taxonomies differ; a majority‑rule LCA can help keep the most frequent annotation.  
- Build a mapping between BOLD IDs and NCBI IDs and replace the taxonomy string of your choice.  
- For common class‑level mismatches, change the BOLD class name to the NCBI equivalent (e.g. Actinopterygii → Actinopteri, Reptilia → Lepidosauria, Elasmobranchii → Chondrichthyes, Copepoda → Hexanauplia, Thecostraca → Hexanauplia).  
- Use VSEARCH’s --p-ranks option to retrieve kingdom, phylum, superclass, order, family, genus and species for each sequence, which can aid in resolving inconsistencies.  
- Depending on the marker gene and available taxonomy, apply a super‑ or sub‑rank approach.  
- Manual curation may still be required because a fully automated merge is difficult.  
- Perform comparative label analysis (e.g., across phylum, class, order, family) to identify systematic differences between the two sources.

instruction: Explain why using --p-rank-handles 'ncbi' or --p-perc-identity 97 in RESCRIPt dereplicate causes errors, and whether dereplication is required for NCBI data.
input: RESCRIPt, dereplicate, --p-rank-handles 'silva', --p-mode 'uniq', --p-rank-handles 'ncbi', --p-perc-identity 97
output: -p-rank-handles sets the expected default prefix style. For the current RESCRIPt implementation it only supports 'silva', 'greengenes', 'gtdb', or 'disable'. It does not support 'ncbi', which is why the command errors. You can either use 'silva' or set it to 'disable' to bypass the prefix handling. Dereplication is optional; it is often recommended to keep the database small and remove redundant information, but it is not mandatory for NCBI data. The option --p-perc-identity 97 is not usable with NCBI data because the data is not formatted for that command; in the COI NCBI tutorial it is used together with --p-mode 'lca' for computed consensus taxonomy.

instruction: Explain restrictions on feature IDs such as underscores and why a KeyError may occur during q2 rescript dereplicate on 16S fragments.
input: q2 rescript dereplicate, q2 feature-classifier extract-reads, qiime2-q2cli-err-2r3fb6vc.log.txt, 16S_015
output: There are restrictions on feature IDs. You should avoid underscores. Older tools often only consider anything before the first underscore as the sequence or sample label. One part of the code trims off anything after the underscore, turning 16S_015 into 16S. When another part of the code looks for 16S_015, it does not find it because it was changed, resulting in the KeyError.

instruction: Use qiime rescript dereplicate with the provided options and explain why the error occurs and how to fix it.
input: qiime rescript dereplicate, i-sequences bold_ambi_hpoly_length_filtd_seqs.qza, i-taxa bold_rawTaxa.qza, p-mode super, p-derep-prefix, p-rank-handles greengenes, o-dereplicated-sequences bold_derep1_seqs.qza, o-dereplicated-taxa bold_derep1_taxa.qza, verbose, error message Plugin error ISSIK304-14
output: The error occurs because the ID ISSIK304-14 is present in the FASTA sequence file but not in the taxonomy file. In the taxonomy file, IDs such as ISSIK304-14-1 or ISSIK304-14_2 exist instead, and the taxonomy file contains many more entries than the sequence file. The plugin cannot find a matching taxonomy for that ID. Ensure that the FASTA and taxonomy files contain identical IDs and that every sequence has a corresponding taxonomy entry before rerunning the dereplicate command. Alternatively, try disabling rank handling with --p-rank-handles 'disable' if you want to skip taxonomy mapping.

instruction: Troubleshoot the qiime vsearch dereplicate-sequences error caused by a broken temporary uc file.
input: qiime, vsearch, list index out of range, /tmp
output: The error was caused by insufficient disk space in the /tmp directory, which resulted in a broken temporary uc file during vsearch dereplication. Changing the TMPDIR environment variable to a location with more space allowed the process to complete. The failure happened in the Python step that parsed the uc file, not during uc file generation itself.

instruction: Explain why the dereplicated files are larger than the original after dereplication with RESCRIPt in QIIME 2.
input: RESCRIPt get-unite-data, RESCRIPt dereplicate, QIIME 2 artifacts sequences.qza, taxonomy.qza
output: File size can be misleading because a QIIME 2 artifact contains provenance information, citations, and other metadata that add to the file size. Even if the sequence content stays the same or shrinks, the artifact can grow because more provenance and metadata are added during processing. In this case, dereplication probably does not change the sequences much because the UNITE data are already clustered (97, 99, or dynamic). Thus the dereplicated output likely contains the same sequences, but the provenance and metadata files increase in size. To see how many sequences you have before and after dereplication, you can use the qiime feature-table tabulate-seqs action or qiime rescript evaluate-seqs.

instruction: Explain how the --p-n-jobs and --p-threads options in QIIME 2 differ in terms of process versus thread parallelism, and how this impacts job execution on a server or HPC environment.
input: --p-n-jobs, --p-threads, qiime feature-classifier classify-sklearn, qiime fragment-insertion sepp
output: The --p-n-jobs option creates separate processes, so each job runs in its own memory space and can be scheduled independently, potentially across multiple nodes if supported. The --p-threads option creates threads that share the same process memory, so all threads run within a single process and cannot be distributed across nodes. Threads are tied to a single node and cannot exceed that node’s CPU cores, while processes are more portable. Currently, both options require a single node large enough for the chosen number of jobs or threads; process-based jobs can in principle be spread across nodes, but this is not yet supported.

instruction: Troubleshoot the error caused by using --p-n-threads 0 or large thread values in qiime dada2 denoise-paired, which reports a vector length mismatch.
input: qiime dada2, --p-n-threads, thread value >22, vector length mismatch
output: This error is a multithreading issue that can be fixed by reducing the number of threads used.

instruction: Use the --p-n-jobs option to parallelize the qiime feature-classifier extract-reads command across multiple CPU cores to reduce processing time.
input: qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, GNU parallel
output: extract-reads already supports parallel execution via a --p-n-jobs parameter. Specify the number of jobs you want to run in parallel (e.g., --p-n-jobs 20). This uses joblib to split the data into chunks and processes them concurrently, dramatically speeding up extraction. A recent QIIME 2 release includes this feature; older releases may not. The documentation can be updated to note this option.

instruction: Explain why the parallelization feature for 'qiime diversity alpha-rarefaction' was removed or dropped in QIIME2, and whether it can now be parallelized.
input: qiime diversity alpha-rarefaction, alpha_rarefaction.py
output: We didn’t have a good way to parallelize it in QIIME 2 when the action was written. We recently added the ability to parallelize actions in a fairly straightforward manner, so there is potential that this action will be parallelized in QIIME 2 (or a different version of the action that is parallelized will be added).
